ToolSpecification.name: recommend
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from awslabs.aws-documentation-mcp-server) Get content recommendations for an AWS documentation page.

    ## Usage

    This tool provides recommendations for related AWS documentation pages based on a given URL.
    Use it to discover additional relevant content that might not appear in search results.

    ## Recommendation Types

    The recommendations include four categories:

    1. **Highly Rated**: Popular pages within the same AWS service
    2. **New**: Recently added pages within the same AWS service - useful for finding newly released features
    3. **Similar**: Pages covering similar topics to the current page
    4. **Journey**: Pages commonly viewed next by other users

    ## When to Use

    - After reading a documentation page to find related content
    - When exploring a new AWS service to discover important pages
    - To find alternative explanations of complex concepts
    - To discover the most popular pages for a service
    - To find newly released information by using a service's welcome page URL and checking the **New** recommendations

    ## Finding New Features

    To find newly released information about a service:
    1. Find any page belong to that service, typically you can try the welcome page
    2. Call this tool with that URL
    3. Look specifically at the **New** recommendation type in the results

    ## Result Interpretation

    Each recommendation includes:
    - url: The documentation page URL
    - title: The page title
    - context: A brief description (if available)

    Args:
        ctx: MCP context for logging and error handling
        url: URL of the AWS documentation page to get recommendations for

    Returns:
        List of recommended pages with URLs, titles, and context

url.title: Url
url.description: URL of the AWS documentation page to get recommendations for

ToolSpecification.name: search_documentation
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from awslabs.aws-documentation-mcp-server) Search AWS documentation using the official AWS Documentation Search API.

    ## Usage

    This tool searches across all AWS documentation for pages matching your search phrase.
    Use it to find relevant documentation when you don't have a specific URL.

    ## Search Tips

    - Use specific technical terms rather than general phrases
    - Include service names to narrow results (e.g., "S3 bucket versioning" instead of just "versioning")
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching (e.g., "AWS Lambda function URLs")
    - Include abbreviations and alternative terms to improve results
    - Use guide_type and product_type filters found from a SearchResponse's "facets" property:
        - Filter only for broad search queries with patterns:
            - "What is [service]?" -> product_types: ["Amazon Simple Storage Service"]
            - "How to use <service 1> with <service 2>?" -> product_types: [<service 1>, <service 2>]
            - "[service] getting started" -> product_types: [<service>] + guide_types: ["User Guide, "Developer Guide"]
            - "API reference for [service]" -> product_types: [<service>] + guide_types: ["API Reference"]

    ## Result Interpretation

    Each SearchResponse includes:
    - search_results: List of documentation pages, each with:
        - rank_order: The relevance ranking (lower is more relevant)
        - url: The documentation page URL
        - title: The page title
        - context: A brief excerpt or summary (if available)
        - sections: Table of contents (when available) - these section titles can be used with the read_sections tool for targeted content extraction
    - facets: Available filters (product_types, guide_types) for refining searches
    - query_id: Unique identifier for this search session


    Args:
        ctx: MCP context for logging and error handling
        search_phrase: Search phrase to use
        search_intent: The intent behind the search requested by the user
        limit: Maximum number of results to return
        product_types: Filter by AWS product/service
        guide_types: Filter by guide type

    Returns:
        List of search results with URLs, titles, query ID, context snippets, and facets for filtering

search_intent.description: For the search_phrase parameter, describe the search intent of the user. CRITICAL: Do not include any PII or customer data, describe only the AWS-related intent for search.
search_intent.title: Search Intent

limit.title: Limit
limit.description: Maximum number of results to return

guide_types.title: Guide Types
guide_types.description: Filter results by guide type (e.g., ["User Guide", "API Reference", "Developer Guide"])

product_types.description: Filter results by AWS product/service (e.g., ["Amazon Simple Storage Service"])
product_types.title: Product Types

ToolSpecification.name: read_documentation
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from awslabs.aws-documentation-mcp-server) Fetch and convert an AWS documentation page to markdown format.

    ## Usage

    This tool retrieves the content of an AWS documentation page and converts it to markdown format.
    For long documents, you can make multiple calls with different start_index values to retrieve
    the entire content in chunks.

    ## URL Requirements

    - Must be from the docs.aws.amazon.com domain
    - Must end with .html

    ## Example URLs

    - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/bucketnamingrules.html
    - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-invocation.html

    ## Output Format

    The output is formatted as markdown text with:
    - Preserved headings and structure
    - Code blocks for examples
    - Lists and tables converted to markdown format

    ## Handling Long Documents

    If the response indicates the document was truncated, you have several options:

    1. **Continue Reading**: Make another call with start_index set to the end of the previous response
    2. **Stop Early**: For very long documents (>30,000 characters), if you've already found the specific information needed, you can stop reading

    Args:
        ctx: MCP context for logging and error handling
        url: URL of the AWS documentation page to read
        max_length: Maximum number of characters to return
        start_index: On return output starting at this character index

    Returns:
        Markdown content of the AWS documentation

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more content is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

url.title: Url
url.description: URL of the AWS documentation page to read

ToolSpecification.name: read_sections
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from awslabs.aws-documentation-mcp-server) Extract specific sections from AWS documentation pages by title.

    Retrieves a page, converts to markdown, and returns only matching sections.
    Section matching is case-insensitive and handles whitespace differences.

    ## URL Requirements
    - Must end with .html

    ## Read Sections Tips

    - Use exact section titles from search results 'sections' field when available
    - Section matching is case-insensitive and handles whitespace differences
    - Include multiple related sections in one call for comprehensive coverage

    ## Example Usage

    ```
    # If query is about S3 bucket naming rules:
    # Available sections: ['General purpose buckets naming rules', 'Example general purpose bucket names', 'Best practices', 'Creating a bucket that uses a GUID in the bucket name']
    # Read these specific sections:
    read_sections(
        url='https://docs.aws.amazon.com/s3/latest/userguide/bucketnamingrules.html',
        section_titles=['General purpose buckets naming rules', 'Best practices'],
    )

    # If query is about Python Lambda function examples:
    # Available sections: ['Example Python Lambda function code', 'Handler naming conventions', 'Using the Lambda event object', 'Accessing and using the Lambda context object'. 'Valid handler signatures for Python handlers', 'Returning a value', 'Using the AWS SDK for Python (Boto3) in your handler', 'Accessing environment variables, 'Code best practices for Python Lambda functions']
    # Read these specific sections:
    read_sections(
        url='https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/python-handler.html',
        section_titles=[
            'Example Python Lambda function code',
            'Code best practices for Python Lambda functions',
        ],
    )
    ```

    Args:
        ctx: MCP context for logging and error handling
        url: URL of the AWS documentation page to read
        section_titles: List of section titles to extract

    Returns:
        Filtered markdown content containing only the requested sections

section_titles.description: List of section titles to extract from the documentation
section_titles.title: Section Titles

url.description: URL of the AWS documentation page to read
url.title: Url

ToolSpecification.name: get_security_context
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from loaf_mcp) MANDATORY: Get security context and best practices, query this tool. This tool must be used before creating/updating ANY aws components.
This tool MUST be called whenever fixing a Shepherd issue for aws.
Existing code may use deprecated or non-compliant patterns. Do not recommend patterns found in existing code without first verifying them against this tool.
⚠️  CRITICAL SECURITY REQUIREMENT ⚠️
    This tool MUST be called before creating ANY AWS resources, regardless of method:
    - CloudFormation templates
    - CDK deployments
    - Terraform configurations
    - Direct AWS API calls
    - AWS CLI commands
    - SDK operations
    - Console operations

    This tool provides essential security-focused configuration guidance, secure defaults, and best practices
    that are REQUIRED for all AWS resource operations. It returns security recommendations and sample
    implementations to ensure AWS resources are created with proper security controls and compliance.

    WHEN TO USE: Always call this tool FIRST before any AWS resource creation task.

    Args:
        keywords: A list of keywords to fetch security context for. Keywords are open-ended
                  and case-insensitive. Unknown keywords return nothing and are ignored.

                  Common AWS service keywords:
                    apigateway, apigateway_v2, autoscaling, certificatemanager, cloudfront,
                    cloudtrail, codebuild, cognito, documentdb, dynamodb, ec2, ecr, ecs, eks,
                    efs, elasticloadbalancingv2, iam, kinesis, lambda, logs, opensearch, rds,
                    s3, sns, sqs, step_function

                  DISCOVERY: If the keyword you need isn't obvious, query a topic name first.
                  Each topic returns a decision tree listing specific keywords to query next.

                  Available topics:
                    authentication   — federation, SSO, midway, sigv4, cloudauth, MAP
                    authorization    — IAM, RBAC, bindles, least-privilege, AVP
                    encryption       — at-rest and in-transit, KMS, certificates, TLS
                    network          — VPC, ELB, API Gateway, ingress/egress controls
                    logging          — CloudWatch, SEL, audit trails
                    secrets          — key and credential rotation, Secrets Manager, ACM
                    coding           — XSS, SSRF, CSRF, CORS, secure deserialization
                    genai            — Bedrock guardrails, secure model deserialization
                    iac              — CDK, CloudFormation, SecureCDK
                    compute          — serverless, AMI, golden images
                    isolate          — environment isolation, SageMaker isolation
                    patching         — AEP, BONES, MFN
                    monitoring       — runtime detection, anomaly, SIEM, canaries
                    continuity       — backup, recovery, PITR
                    supply_chain     — dependencies, 3P, OSS, Brazil, Peru
                    resilience       — DDoS, throttling, NoiseGate

                  Query multiple keywords per call. Topics are not exhaustive — if you see a
                  specific Amazon security tool name in source files (e.g., "merlon", "brass",
                  "horizonte", "superstar"), query it directly.
---

keywords.description: Each keyword should be a single word or underscore-separated identifier, e.g. ['s3', 'authentication', 'cloudfront', 'secure_http_headers', 'mobile_network']

⚠️  CRITICAL SECURITY REQUIREMENT ⚠️

ToolSpecification.description:

Use di_search_stencils for other services. Returns error with suggestions if type not found.

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_data_handling
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a data handling (information asset) entry from a component in a Design Inspector diagram. Handles both catalog assets (by IRI) and custom data elements (by label). Pass the asset IRI for catalog assets, or the label string for custom data elements. Other data handling entries on the same component are not affected.

asset_identifier.description: The asset IRI to remove (for catalog assets) or the label string (for custom data elements).

page.description: Page name to update (defaults to first page).

component_id.description: Component ID as it appears in the diagram (the id attribute on the component element).

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_custom_security_property
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Set a custom ("Other") freetext value for a security property on a component. Use this when the predefined options from di_get_component_security_properties do not fit and a custom value is needed (e.g., a custom authentication mechanism). The value is stored as a nested ResourceDescriptor with an rdfs:label StringLiteral in the component SemanticDataContainer. IMPORTANT: You MUST call di_get_component_security_properties first to look up the valid property_iri for this component. Setting the same property twice replaces the previous value (whether IRI or custom).

component_id.description: Component ID as it appears in the diagram (the id attribute on the component element).

page.description: Page name to update (defaults to first page).

property_iri.description: Property predicate IRI to set, obtained from di_get_component_security_properties (the property_iri field in available_properties).

custom_value.description: The custom freetext value to store (max 250 characters, e.g., "Custom Auth Mechanism").

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_cryptography

data_types.description: JSON array of data types: [{id, displayName, classificationName, description?}]. Use di_list_component_data_types to discover valid values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_property
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Set a property on a component in a Design Inspector diagram. Supports "purpose" (Intended Purpose field) and "description" (Description field, stored as rdfs:comment).

ToolSpecification.name: di_apply_edge_routes
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Apply computed edge routes (port assignments, waypoints, and/or styles) to existing edges in a diagram for cleaner layout. Each route specifies a source→target edge and optionally: exit/entry port positions (0–1 normalized), waypoints, and composable style options. Port values: (0,0.5)=left, (1,0.5)=right, (0.5,0)=top, (0.5,1)=bottom. Edges are matched by source+target IDs. Includes validation for port ranges, endpoint existence, and edge existence. Style is composable: choose routing (orthogonal/straight/curved) and layer visual modifiers (dashed, color). Defaults to orthogonal routing. Use thick and bidirectional only when truly necessary — most edges should be normal weight, single-direction. OVERLAP AVOIDANCE: When a component has multiple edges on the same side, spread exit/entry ports evenly along that side to prevent overlapping edges. For example, 3 edges exiting the right side should use exitY values like 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 instead of all using 0.5. For long edges that cross over other components, use waypoints to route around them — e.g., route an edge above or below congested areas with intermediate waypoints. Avoid having multiple edges share the same exact port coordinates on the same component. TIP: Use di_get_diagram first to see existing edge info (id, label, source, target, style) so you can plan optimal port assignments and avoid conflicts.

style.description: Composable edge style options. Combine routing (orthogonal/straight/curved) with visual modifiers (dashed, thick, bidirectional, color). All modifiers can be combined freely — e.g., { routing: "curved", dashed: true, color: "#FF0000" }.

color.description: Stroke color as hex (e.g., "#FF0000" for red)

routing.description: Edge routing algorithm: orthogonal (right-angle bends), straight (direct line), curved

routes.description: Array of edge routes to apply, each with optional per-edge style

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_use_case
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a new use case to the Threat Model document.

role_name.description: Role name this use case belongs to

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_data_handling
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a data handling (information asset) entry to a component in a Design Inspector diagram. The asset is stored as an IRI tag in the component SemanticDataContainer. IMPORTANT: Call di_list_data_elements first to find valid asset_iri values. A component can have multiple data handling entries (append semantics). Setting the same asset twice is a no-op (duplicate detection).

page.description: Page name to update (defaults to first page).

component_id.description: Component ID as it appears in the diagram (the id attribute on the component element).

asset_iri.description: Full asset IRI from di_list_data_elements (the iri field in the results).

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_organizations
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List available organizations for Design Inspector diagrams. Returns organization IDs and names that can be used when creating diagrams with the organization field.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_use_cases
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all use cases from the Threat Model document.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_roles
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all roles and their entry points from the Threat Model document.

ToolSpecification.name: di_get_all_threats
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Get ALL threats for a diagram by triggering a scan, polling for completion, then merging automated findings and custom threats with their status (UNMITIGATED/MITIGATED/FALSE_POSITIVE). This may take 30-60 seconds as it waits for the scan to complete.

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_credential
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a credential by ID. To update, remove it and add a new one with the updated values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_puffball_query_triples
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Query Puffball using Triple Pattern Fragments (TPF). Specify at least one of subject, predicate, or object to filter RDF triples.

Use di_puffball_query_path for the overview of an IRI in the Knowledge Graph. Use this tool when want to go deep on a part of an entity. 
WHEN TO USE THIS TOOL:
- Use when you need to find specific relationships in the knowledge graph
- Use when you know the subject IRI and want to find all its properties
- Use when you know a predicate and want to find all subject-object pairs using it
- Use when you need to find all resources with a specific value (object)

PREFER OTHER TOOLS WHEN:
- Need to explore the full ontology at a path for a component or assets, or other entities → use di_puffball_query_path
- Need human-readable component names → use di_puffball_all_preflabels
- Need security properties for a specific component → use di_get_component_security_properties
- Need to search for stencils by keyword → use di_search_stencils

PARAMETER USAGE:
- subject: Full IRI of the resource (e.g., "https://ontology.security.amazon.dev/design-inspector/components/xxx")
- predicate: Property IRI (e.g., "http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#prefLabel", "http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type")
- object: Value or target resource IRI
- basePath: Ontology path to query within (default: /design-inspector/components)

COMMON PREDICATES:
- skos:prefLabel - Human-readable name
- rdf:type - Class membership
- rdfs:subClassOf - Class hierarchy
- rdfs:comment - Description text

subject.description: Filter by subject URI (the resource being described)

object.description: Filter by object (the value or target resource)

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_component_visual_only
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove the visual-only marking from an unmodeled component, reverting it to a Normal System Component. The component will be included in threat modeling and security scans again.

component_id.description: Component ID to remove visual-only from

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_security_property
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Set a security or additional property on a component in a Design Inspector diagram. The property is stored as an IRI tag in the component SemanticDataContainer. IMPORTANT: You MUST call di_get_component_security_properties first to look up valid property_iri and option_iri values for this component. The component must have semantic metadata (i.e. have been added via DI or di_add_component). Setting the same property twice replaces the previous value.

component_id.description: Component ID as it appears in the diagram (the id attribute on the component element).

page.description: Page name to update (defaults to first page).

property_iri.description: Property predicate IRI to set, obtained from di_get_component_security_properties (the property_iri field in available_properties).

option_iri.description: Option value IRI to assign, obtained from di_get_component_security_properties (the option_iri field inside available_options).

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_log_management
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a log management entry by ID. To update, remove it and add a new one with the updated values.

log_management_id.description: Log management entry ID to remove

ToolSpecification.name: di_puffball_all_preflabels
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Get all subjects and their prefLabels from the Puffball Knowledge Graph. This tool is useful when needs to know the IRI of a component given the name of it.
Queries for all triples with the skos:prefLabel predicate and returns subject/label pairs.

WHEN TO USE THIS TOOL:
- Use when you need to fuzzy search/filter on component labels to find a component IRI
- Use when you have a partial or approximate component name and need to find the exact IRI
- Use when other search tools (di_search_stencils) do not return the component you need

PREFER OTHER TOOLS WHEN:
- Need stencil types for diagrams → use di_search_stencils or di_list_stencils
- Need security properties for a component → use di_get_component_security_properties
- Need to query specific relationships → use di_puffball_query_triples
- Need to explore ontology structure → use di_puffball_query_path

TYPICAL WORKFLOW:
1. Call this tool to get all label/IRI pairs
2. Search/filter the results locally for matching labels
3. Use the found IRI with other tools like di_get_component_security_properties

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_components
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all components and edges in a Design Inspector diagram. Useful for getting component IDs and component IRIs for other operations such as setting security properties.

page.description: Filter by page name (shows all pages if not specified)

ToolSpecification.name: di_validate_diagram
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Validate diagram structure before updating. Checks: well-formed XML, mxGraphModel/root structure, base/layer cell hierarchy, no duplicate element IDs, ResourceDescriptor integrity (resource attribute, non-empty SemanticDataContainer, property attributes on IRI/StringLiteral). Use this proactively before di_update_diagram with manually edited XML, or diagnostically when a diagram-modifying tool returns a "validation failed" error.

page.description: Page name to validate (validates all pages if not specified)

ToolSpecification.name: di_create_diagram
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Create a new Design Inspector diagram. Provide either an XML file path with decoded mxGraph XML, or a blank diagram will be created.

xml_file.description: Path to decoded XML file (optional - creates blank diagram if not provided)

bindle_id.description: Bindle ID to store the diagram (use di_list_bindles to find available bindles)

organization.description: The Full IRI of Organization identifier (use di_list_organizations to find available organizations, defaults to empty if not provided)

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_cryptography
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all cryptography entries from the Threat Model document.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_stencils
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List available stencil types. By default returns only category names with counts (lightweight). Use category param to get full list for one category. Example: di_list_stencils() → categories overview, di_list_stencils(category="aws_database") → all database stencils.

summary.description: If true with category, return only stencil names (no metadata)

category.description: Get full stencil list for this category (e.g., "aws_compute", "aws_database", "aws_security")

ToolSpecification.name: di_get_component_security_properties
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all available security and additional properties (and their selectable options) for a component, identified by its Puffball Knowledge Graph IRI. Results are cached locally for 24 hours to avoid repeated Puffball API calls. ALWAYS call this tool first before calling di_set_security_property to discover valid property_iri and option_iri values for the target component. NOTE: Only supported for diagrams belonging to the SDO organization.

diagram.description: Diagram name — used to verify the diagram belongs to the SDO organization.

component_iri.description: Full component IRI from the Puffball Knowledge Graph (e.g. "https://ontology.security.amazon.dev/design-inspector/components/e6211311-da32-4e88-ab21-00d1660b26e0"). Obtainable from di_list_components (the componentIri field) or di_puffball_all_preflabels.

force_refresh.description: If true, bypass the local cache and fetch fresh data from Puffball (default: false).

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_component_as_dependency
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Mark a component as a Dependent/Dependency Component. Sets the component threat model status to "Dependent/Dependency Component" with a required ownership type. Works on both modeled and unmodeled components. If the component was previously marked as visual-only, that marking is removed.

component_id.description: Component ID to mark as dependency

ownership.description: Component ownership: AMAZON (Amazon owned), CUSTOMER_OWNED (Customer owned), THIRD_PARTY (Third-party owned)

ToolSpecification.name: di_update_diagram
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Update an existing Design Inspector diagram from a local XML file. Automatically fetches current version for optimistic locking.

organization.description: The Full IRI of Organization identifier to update to (use di_list_organizations to find available organizations, defaults to current organization if not provided)

xml_file.description: Path to decoded XML file with updates

ToolSpecification.name: di_search_stencils
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Search for stencils by keyword. Searches type names and categories. Example: di_search_stencils("database") → dynamodb, rds, aurora, etc. Use this when you need a specific service but don't know the exact stencil name.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_threats
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all custom threats for a Design Inspector diagram.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_credentials
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all credentials from the Threat Model document.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_bindles
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List all Design Inspector bindles accessible to the current user. Returns bindle names and IDs for use when creating diagrams.

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_role
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a new role to the Threat Model document. Privileges is an optional JSON array of {action, componentId, entryPoints[]} objects.

privileges.description: JSON array of privileges: [{action, componentId, entryPoints: []}]

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_custom_data_handling
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a custom data handling element to a component in a Design Inspector diagram. Use this when the data element is NOT in the asset catalog (di_list_data_elements). Creates a nested ResourceDescriptor with a label, definition (min 10 chars), and classification. Valid classification values: PUBLIC, CONFIDENTIAL, RESTRICTED, HIGHLY_CONFIDENTIAL, CRITICAL. A component can have multiple data handling entries (append semantics). Adding a custom element with the same label twice is a no-op (duplicate detection).

label.description: Display name for the custom data element (e.g., "User Session Token"). Max 1000 characters.

component_id.description: Component ID as it appears in the diagram (the id attribute on the component element).

definition.description: Description/definition for the custom data element (e.g., "JWT token used for user auth"). Min 10, max 5000 characters.

classification.description: Data classification level. Valid values: PUBLIC, CONFIDENTIAL, RESTRICTED, HIGHLY_CONFIDENTIAL, CRITICAL.

page.description: Page name to update (defaults to first page).

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_role
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a role by ID. To update a role, remove it and add a new one with the updated values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_get_general_info
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Get the application URL or Documentation link and architecture page name from the Threat Model document.

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_credential
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a credential to the Threat Model document.

rotation_frequency.description: How often the credential is rotated

component_id.description: Component ID this credential belongs to

ToolSpecification.name: di_delete_component
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Delete a component from a Design Inspector diagram by its ID. Also removes any edges connected to the deleted component. Use di_list_components to find component IDs.

component_id.description: ID of the component to delete (from the id attribute on the object/mxCell)

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_cryptography
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a cryptography entry by ID. To update, remove it and add a new one with the updated values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_set_general_info
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Set the application URL or Documentation link and architecture page name in the Threat Model document. The page id and name are resolved from the actual diagram XML. If page_name is provided, it must match an existing page in the diagram. If omitted, the first page is used.

page_name.description: Page name to use as the architecture diagram page (must match a page in the diagram). Defaults to the first page.

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_data_elements
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List available data elements (information assets) from the Design Inspector knowledge graph. Use this to discover asset IRIs before calling di_set_data_handling. Results are cached for 24 hours. Use force_refresh to bypass the cache. Use the search parameter to filter by label (case-insensitive substring match).

force_refresh.description: Bypass cache and fetch fresh data from Puffball.

ToolSpecification.name: di_delete_edge
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Delete an edge from a Design Inspector diagram by its ID. Use di_list_components to find edge IDs.

edge_id.description: ID of the edge to delete (from the id attribute on the object/mxCell)

ToolSpecification.name: di_list_component_data_types
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) List the data types (information assets) associated with a component from the threat model document. Use this to discover valid dataTypes values before adding cryptography or log management entries.

ToolSpecification.name: di_puffball_query_path
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Query the Puffball Knowledge Graph by ontology path for a component, asset or other data. Returns RDF data about Design Inspector components and ontology.

WHEN TO USE THIS TOOL:
- Use when you need to see all triples where the current path (component, asset and etc) is either subject, predicate or object.
- Use when you need to explore the full ontology structure od a specific component/asset under the path
- Use when you need to understand what classes, properties, or instances exist in a domain
- Use for broad discovery of available data in a knowledge graph namespace

PREFER OTHER TOOLS WHEN:
- Need specific security properties details for a component → use di_get_component_security_properties
- Need to find components by name → use di_puffball_all_preflabels or di_search_stencils
- Need to filter by subject/predicate/object → use di_puffball_query_triples

COMMON PATHS:
- "/design-inspector/components" - AWS service component definitions and security properties
- "/design-inspector/stencils" - Diagram stencil types for visual representation
- "/asset-catalog/assets" - Information asset types and classifications
- "/foundation" - Core ontology concepts (assets, controls, requirements)

FORMAT OPTIONS:
- turtle (default): Best for parsing RDF triples, most compact
- json-ld: Best when JSON structure is preferred
- html: Human-readable documentation format
- plain: Raw text output

format.description: Response format: turtle (default), json-ld, html, or plain

ToolSpecification.name: di_quality_findings
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Analyze a Design Inspector diagram for quality issues without triggering a scan. Performs client-side checks matching the DI UI Findings panel: missing data handling (BLOCKING/WARNING), missing actor on architecture page (WARNING), missing component descriptions (WARNING), disconnected edges (WARNING), and unset security properties (WARNING). Returns findings grouped by type with severity, affected component IDs, and summary counts. Use this as a quick pre-check before running a full di_get_all_threats.

include_security_properties.description: Check for unset security properties (requires Puffball API calls per component, slower). Default: false.

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_use_case
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove a use case by ID. To update, remove it and add a new one with the updated values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_create_document
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Create a threat model document for an existing Design Inspector diagram. This uses browser automation to interact with the DI UI, which ensures the document is properly synced with the diagram (components, data classification, etc.). Requires: the diagram must already exist in DI, `mwinit` must have been run, and `mcscli` must be installed (part of midway-client-suite).

diagram_name.description: Name of the existing DI diagram to create a threat model for

ToolSpecification.name: di_open_diagram
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Open a Design Inspector diagram in the default web browser.

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_log_management
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a log management entry to the Threat Model document.

data_types.description: JSON array of data types: [{id, displayName, classificationName, description?}]. Use di_list_component_data_types to discover valid values.

ToolSpecification.name: di_scan
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Trigger a security scan on a Design Inspector diagram. Returns scan job ID for tracking.

ToolSpecification.name: di_remove_component_dependency
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Remove the dependency marking from a component, reverting it to a Normal System Component. Removes both the external component type and ownership metadata.

component_id.description: Component ID to remove dependency from

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_edge
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a connection/edge between two components. Defaults to orthogonal (right-angle) routing — prefer plain default edges for most connections. Style guidelines (use sparingly): • Use dashed lines for error paths, async/optional flows, or infrastructure relationships. • Use color to distinguish edge categories when helpful: e.g., red (#FF0000) for error/DLQ paths, blue (#0000FF) for status updates. • Use curved routing for edges that would otherwise overlap with orthogonal routing. • Use thick and bidirectional ONLY when truly necessary (e.g., a critical primary flow or a genuine read/write relationship) — most edges should be normal weight, single-direction. Edge style is composable — combine routing with visual modifiers: { style: { routing: "orthogonal", dashed: true, color: "#FF0000" } }. Optional port parameters (exitX/exitY, entryX/entryY) control where the edge connects on the source/target component. Port values are normalized 0–1: (0,0.5)=left, (1,0.5)=right, (0.5,0)=top, (0.5,1)=bottom. OVERLAP AVOIDANCE: When a component has multiple edges on the same side, spread exit/entry ports evenly along that side to prevent overlapping edges. For example, 3 edges exiting the right side should use exitY values like 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 instead of all using 0.5. For long edges that cross over other components, use waypoints or choose port positions that route around congested areas. Avoid having multiple edges share the same exact port coordinates on the same component. IMPORTANT: After adding all edges, call di_apply_edge_routes to optimize port assignments (exit/entry points) and waypoints for cleaner edge routing and better visualization.

routing.description: Edge routing algorithm: orthogonal (right-angle bends), straight (direct line), curved

color.description: Stroke color as hex (e.g., "#FF0000" for red)

style.description: Composable edge style options. Combine routing (orthogonal/straight/curved) with visual modifiers (dashed, thick, bidirectional, color). All modifiers can be combined freely — e.g., { routing: "curved", dashed: true, color: "#FF0000" }.

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_region
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a colored background region/box to visually group components. Regions are placed behind components.

ToolSpecification.name: di_get_diagram
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Get a Design Inspector diagram by name. Automatically decodes the XML and saves it to a local file. Returns diagram metadata, component list, edge list (with id, label, source, target, style for each edge), and file path. Use this to understand the current diagram structure including edge connections, labels, and styles before modifying edges or applying edge routes.

output_dir.description: Directory to save decoded XML (defaults to current directory)

ToolSpecification.name: di_add_threat
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from design-inspector-mcp) Add a custom security threat to a Design Inspector diagram.

severity.description: Severity level: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, or INFO

component_ids.description: Array of component IDs affected by this threat

ToolSpecification.name: web_search
ToolSpecification.description:
WebSearch looks up information that is outside the model's training data or cannot be reliably inferred from the current codebase/context.
Tool performs basic compliance wrt content licensing and restriction.
As an agent you are responsible for adhering to compliance and attribution requirements.
IMPORTANT: The snippets often contain enough information to answer questions - only use web_fetch if you need more detailed content from a specific webpage.

## When to Use
- When the user asks for current or up-to-date information (e.g., pricing, versions, technical specs) or explicitly requests a web search.
- When verifying information that may have changed recently, or when the user provides a specific URL to inspect.

## When NOT to Use
- When the question involves basic concepts, historical facts, or well-established programming syntax/technical documentation.
- When the topic does not require current or evolving information.

For any code-related tasks, follow this order:
1. Search within the repository (if tools are available) and check if it can be inferred from existing code or documentation.
2. Use this tool only if still unresolved and the library/data is likely new/unseen.

## Content Compliance Requirements
You MUST adhere to strict licensing restrictions and attribution requirements when using search results:

### Attribution Requirements
- ALWAYS provide inline links to original sources using format: [description](url)
- If not possible to provide inline link, add sources at the end of file
- Ensure attribution is visible and accessible

### Verbatim Reproduction Limits
- NEVER reproduce more than 30 consecutive words from any single source
- Track word count per source to ensure compliance
- Always paraphrase and summarize rather than quote directly
- Add compliance note when the content from the source is rephrased: "Content was rephrased for compliance with licensing restrictions"

### Content Modification Guidelines
- You MAY paraphrase, summarize, and reformat content
- You MUST NOT materially change the underlying substance or meaning
- Preserve factual accuracy while condensing information
- Avoid altering core arguments, data, or conclusions

## Usage Details
- You may rephrase user queries to improve search effectiveness
- You can make multiple queries to gather comprehensive information
- Consider breaking complex questions into focused searches
- Refine queries based on initial results if needed

## Output Usage
- Prioritize latest published sources based on publishedDate
- Prefer official documentation to blogs and news posts
- Use domain information to assess source authority and reliability

## Error Handling
- If unable to comply with content restrictions, explain limitations to user
- Suggest alternative approaches when content cannot be reproduced
- Prioritize compliance over completeness when conflicts arise

## Output
The tool returns a JSON object with a "results" array containing search results:

{
  "results": [
    {
      "title": "Example Page Title",
      "url": "https://example.com/page",
      "snippet": "Brief excerpt from the page...",
      "publishedDate": "2025-11-20T10:30:00Z",
      "domain": "example.com",
      "id": "unique-id-123",
      "maxVerbatimWordLimit": 30,
      "publicDomain": false
    }
  ]
}

## UI FROM LLM (You) back to the user
CRITICAL: Always start your response with "Here's what I found:" and then start from a newline.
ALWAYS end your response with a blank line followed by 'References:' and list the sources you used in sequential order [1], [2], [3], etc. with NO gaps in numbering. Format: '[N] Title - URL' one per line. Truncate long titles to 80 characters and long URLs to 100 characters, adding '...' if truncated.

query.description: Search query (max 200 chars) - use concise keywords, not full sentences

ToolSpecification.name: grep
ToolSpecification.description:
Fast text pattern search in files using regex. ALWAYS use this tool instead of 'grep', 'rg', or 'ag' commands in bash. Respects .gitignore.

## Text Discovery Only
Use grep for literal text/pattern matching: error messages, TODOs, config values, regex patterns.

## For Semantic Code Understanding → Use 'code' tool if available
- Finding symbol definitions or usages → code tool (search_symbols, goto_definition, find_references)
- Understanding code structure/relationships → code tool
- Distinguishing definition vs call vs import → code tool

## Fallback
If the 'code' tool is available but returns insufficient symbol info, use grep to discover candidate files/lines, then return to 'code' for precise navigation.

When you use this tool, prefer to show the user a small list of representative matches (including file paths and line numbers) instead of only giving a high-level summary.

path.description: Directory to search from. Defaults to current working directory.

max_depth.description: Max directory depth to traverse when searching (search limit). Increase for deeply nested structures.

max_files.description: Max number of files returned (output limit). Increase for comprehensive codebase searches.

max_total_lines.description: Max total matched lines returned across all files (output limit). Increase when searching for many occurrences.

output_mode.description: Output format: 'content' returns matches as 'file:line:content' (default, best for seeing actual matches), 'files_with_matches' returns only file paths, 'count' returns match counts per file.

pattern.description: Regex pattern to search for. Examples: 'fn main', 'class.*Component', 'TODO|FIXME'. Start with simple patterns first (e.g. just the word you're looking for), then refine if needed.

max_matches_per_file.description: Max matches returned per file (output limit). Increase to see all occurrences in a file.

ToolSpecification.name: use_aws
ToolSpecification.description: Make an AWS CLI api call with the specified service, operation, and parameters. All arguments MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. Should the output of the invocation indicate a malformed command, invoke help to obtain the the correct command.

operation_name.description: The name of the operation to perform.

profile_name.description: Optional: AWS profile name to use from ~/.aws/credentials. Defaults to default profile if not specified.

positional_args.description: Positional arguments for high-level commands (e.g., s3 cp, s3 mv, s3 sync, s3 rm). These are passed directly without -- prefix. Use this for source/destination paths in S3 commands.

label.description: Human readable description of the api that is being called.

parameters.description: The parameters for the operation. The parameter keys MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. You should prefer to use JSON Syntax over shorthand syntax wherever possible. For parameters that are booleans, prioritize using flags with no value. Denote these flags with flag names as key and an empty string as their value. You should also prefer kebab case.

service_name.description: The name of the AWS service. If you want to query s3, you should use s3api if possible. Must not start with a dash (-).

region.description: Region name for calling the operation on AWS.

ToolSpecification.name: introspect
ToolSpecification.description: Use ONLY when the user is asking about this chat application's own features, slash commands, settings, or capabilities. Do NOT use for general coding questions, AWS help, or tasks the user wants you to perform. When mentioning commands in your response, always prefix them with '/' (e.g., '/chat save', '/chat load', '/context'). CRITICAL: Only provide information explicitly documented. If details about any tool, feature, or command are not documented, clearly state the information is not available rather than generating assumptions.

doc_path.description: Path to a specific doc to retrieve (e.g., "features/tangent-mode.md"). Use this to get full content of a doc from the index.

query.description: The user's question about this assistant's usage, features, or capabilities

ToolSpecification.name: report_issue
ToolSpecification.description: Opens the browser to a pre-filled gh (GitHub) issue template to report chat issues, bugs, or feature requests. Pre-filled information includes the conversation transcript, chat context, and chat request IDs from the service.

steps_to_reproduce.description: Optional: Previous user chat requests or steps that were taken that may have resulted in the issue or error response.

expected_behavior.description: Optional: The expected chat behavior or action that did not happen.

actual_behavior.description: Optional: The actual chat behavior that happened and demonstrates the issue or lack of a feature.

title.description: The title of the GitHub issue.

ToolSpecification.name: use_subagent
ToolSpecification.description:
⚠️ CRITICAL DELEGATION TOOL ⚠️

🔍 BEFORE attempting ANY task, CHECK if you have the required tools in YOUR current tool list.

❌ If you DON'T have the necessary tools → YOU MUST use this tool to delegate to a subagent that does.
✅ If you DO have the tools → Handle the task yourself.

## When to Use (MANDATORY scenarios):

1. **MISSING TOOLS**: The user asks you to do something but you don't see the required tool in your available tools list
   - Example: User asks to read a file, but you don't have 'fs_read' → USE THIS TOOL
   - Example: User asks to search code, but you don't have 'code' tool → USE THIS TOOL
   - Example: User asks to run bash command, but you don't have 'execute_bash' → USE THIS TOOL

2. **PARALLEL PROCESSING**: A complex task can be split into independent subtasks that different specialized agents can handle simultaneously

3. **CAPABILITY CHECK**: Use ListAgents command first to see what specialized agents and their toolsets are available

## @prompt References in Queries

You CAN pass `@prompt-name` references (including arguments) directly in the subagent query field. The system automatically resolves @prompt references before the subagent starts — the prompt content is expanded inline and the subagent receives the fully resolved text. Do NOT refuse to delegate because a query contains `@prompt-name` syntax.

Example: `{"query": "@my-task 'arg1:value1, arg2:value2'"}` — this is valid and will be resolved.

## How Subagents Are Different:
- Subagents have DIFFERENT, SPECIALIZED toolsets than you
- Each subagent may have tools you don't have access to
- They operate independently with their own context
- Up to 4 subagents can work in parallel

## Decision Flow:
```
User makes request → Check YOUR tools list → Missing required tool? → USE use_subagent
                                          → Have required tool? → Handle it yourself
```

⚡ Remember: Don't apologize about lacking tools - just delegate to a subagent that has them! Also note that subagents that are spawned together could not communicate with each other. If they are to perform tasks that are dependent on each other. Spawn them with a different tool call!

subagents.description: Array of subagent invocations to execute in parallel. Each invocation specifies a query, optional agent name, and optional context.

agent_name.description: Optional name of the specific agent to use. If not provided, uses the default agent

relevant_context.description: Optional additional context that should be provided to the subagent to help it understand the task better

query.description: The query or task to be handled by the subagent

content.description: Required for `InvokeSubagents` command. Contains subagents array and optional conversation ID.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are `ListAgents` to query available agents, or `InvokeSubagents` to invoke one or more subagents

ToolSpecification.name: fs_read
ToolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading files, directories and images. Always provide an 'operations' array.

For single operation: provide array with one element.
For batch operations: provide array with multiple elements.

Available modes:
- Line: Read lines from a file
- Directory: List directory contents
- Search: Search for patterns in files
- Image: Read and process images

Examples:
1. Single: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file.txt"}]}
2. Batch: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file1.txt"}, {"mode": "Search", "path": "/file2.txt", "pattern": "test"}]}

summary.description: Optional description of the purpose of this batch operation (mainly useful for multiple operations)

operations.description: Array of operations to execute. Provide one element for single operation, multiple for batch.

start_line.description: Starting line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

offset.description: Number of entries to skip for pagination (optional, for Directory mode). Use with max_entries to iterate through large directories. Entries are sorted by last modified time (most recent first). Default: 0

path.description: Path to the file or directory. The path should be absolute, or otherwise start with ~ for the user's home (required for Line, Directory, Search modes).

exclude_patterns.description: Glob patterns to exclude from directory listing (optional, for Directory mode). If omitted, uses defaults. If empty array [] is provided, no exclusions are applied (shows everything). If patterns are provided, they completely override the defaults. Examples: '**/target/**', '*.log'

image_paths.description: List of paths to the images. This is currently supported by the Image mode.

end_line.description: Ending line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

depth.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional, for Directory mode)

mode.description: The operation mode to run in: `Line`, `Directory`, `Search`. `Line` and `Search` are only for text files, and `Directory` is only for directories. `Image` is for image files, in this mode `image_paths` is required.

pattern.description: Pattern to search for (required, for Search mode). Case insensitive. The pattern matching is performed per line.

context_lines.description: Number of context lines around search results (optional, for Search mode)

max_entries.description: Maximum number of entries to return (optional, for Directory mode). When limit is reached, results are truncated and metadata shows 'showing X of Y entries'. Use to prevent context window overflow. Default: 1000

ToolSpecification.name: glob
ToolSpecification.description: Find files and directories whose paths match a glob pattern. Respects .gitignore. Prefer this over the bash 'find' command for listing or discovering paths. Returns JSON with totalFiles (count found), truncated (true if limited), and filePaths array. When truncated is true, just mention results are truncated, don't state the limit number.

limit.description: Maximum files to return. If totalFiles exceeds this, truncated will be true.

path.description: Root directory to search from. Only set this when the user explicitly mentions a directory path. In all other cases, omit this so the tool searches from the current working directory (the project root).

max_depth.description: Maximum directory depth to traverse. Increase for deep nested structures.

ToolSpecification.name: fs_write
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for creating and editing files
 * The `create` command will override the file at `path` if it already exists as a file, and otherwise create a new file
 * The `append` command will add content to the end of an existing file, automatically adding a newline if the file doesn't end with one. The file must exist.
 Notes for using the `str_replace` command:
 * The `old_str` parameter should match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the original file. Be mindful of whitespaces!
 * If the `old_str` parameter is not unique in the file, the replacement will not be performed. Make sure to include enough context in `old_str` to make it unique
 * The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that should replace the `old_str`.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `append`.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string. Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert. Required parameter of `append` command containing the content to append to the file.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the file change does or why it's being made.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/repo/file.py` or `/repo`.

ToolSpecification.name: execute_bash
ToolSpecification.description: Execute the specified bash command. NEVER prefix commands with cd to change the working directory, use the `working_dir` argument instead.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the command does

working_dir.description: Working directory for command execution. Supports tilde expansion (e.g., ~/projects). If not specified, uses the current working directory.

ToolSpecification.name: code
ToolSpecification.description:
Code intelligence to analyze code with AST parsing and fuzzy search. Language auto-detected from file extension.

IMPORTANT: Prefer this tool over fs_read for code files. This tool intelligently provides structured code analysis.

CORE FEATURES:
• Fuzzy search for symbols (classes, functions, methods)
• Extracts function/class signatures via AST
• Structural AST search and rewrite (ast-grep)
• Codebase overview and directory exploration

NOTE: Use fs_read with line ranges for unsupported patterns.

## Operations

**search_symbols** - Find symbol definitions by name across workspace.
Params: symbol_name (required), path, symbol_type, limit, language, exact_match
Uses fuzzy matching by default. Set exact_match=true for precise matching.

**lookup_symbols** - Batch lookup specific symbols (max 10).
Params: symbols (required), file_path, include_source
Set include_source=false for large classes/structs to avoid context overflow.
Scoped lookup: Provide file_path to search within specific file only.

**get_document_symbols** - List all symbols in a file.
Params: file_path (required), top_level_only (recommended: true)
Use this for understanding file structure without reading content.
Prefer this over fs_read when you need symbol information.

**pattern_search** - AST-based structural search using ast-grep.
Params: pattern, language (required), file_path, limit

**pattern_rewrite** - AST-based code transformation.
Params: pattern, replacement, language (required), file_path, limit, dry_run
Use dry_run=true first to preview changes.

## Pattern Search & Rewrite

Metavariables:
• $VAR - Matches single node (identifier, expression)
• $$$ - Matches zero or more nodes (statements, parameters)

Simple patterns:
• console.log($ARG) - Match any console.log call
• function $NAME($$$PARAMS) {} - Match function declaration
• $OBJ.$METHOD() - Match any method call

Structural rules (YAML):
• pattern - Match code structure
• kind - Match AST node type (function_declaration, class_declaration)
• has - Node contains descendant matching pattern
• inside - Node is inside ancestor matching pattern
• all/any/not - Logical operators

Critical: For relational rules (has, inside), always use stopBy: end

Rewrite examples:
• pattern='var $N = $V', replacement='const $N = $V'
• pattern='$O.hasOwnProperty($P)', replacement='Object.hasOwn($O, $P)'

Workflow:
1. Use pattern_search first to verify matches
2. Review matches to ensure correctness
3. Test rewrite on small subset
4. Apply to full codebase after verification

**generate_codebase_overview** - ONLY for explicit architecture questions. Not for bug fixes or finding code.
Params: path (optional)

**search_codebase_map** - Focused directory exploration.
Params: file_path, path

## Explore Unfamiliar Code
1. search_symbols for domain-specific types/functions/methods/classes
2. lookup_symbols with include_source=true on promising matches
3. grep (max 2 searches) for literal text patterns
4. Repeat 1-3 with refined terms if needed
5. generate_codebase_overview ONLY for architecture review or full codebase understanding
   - NOT for bug fixes, feature work, or finding specific code

## Tool Selection Priority
1. search_symbols or lookup_symbols for finding code - ALWAYS use for functions/methods/classes/structs/interfaces
2. get_document_symbols for file structure
3. grep - ONLY for literal text in comments/strings, config values, code
4. fs_read - raw file content when needed

## CRITICAL RULES
- Start with search_symbols
- Always follow search_symbols with lookup_symbols
- When searching for multiple related symbols, call search_symbols in parallel

## Quick Reference
• "What's in this file?" → get_document_symbols
• "Show me X class" → search_symbols, then lookup_symbol

## LSP Operations (Not Available)
These require LSP initialization (/code init):
find_references, goto_definition, get_hover, get_completions, get_diagnostics, rename_symbol

symbols.description: List of simple symbol names, not qualified (e.g. ['myFunction'] not ['MyClass.myFunction']). Required for lookup_symbols, max 10

symbol_name.description: Simple symbol name, not qualified (e.g. 'myFunction' not 'MyClass.myFunction'). Required for search_symbols

include_source.description: Include source code in results (optional for lookup_symbols)

path.description: Directory path (optional, for generate_codebase_overview: workspace root, for search_codebase_map: path filter, for search_symbols: scope search to file or directory)

language.description: Programming language (required for pattern_search, optional for search_symbols)

operation.description: The code intelligence operation to perform

file_path.description: File path (required for get_document_symbols, optional for pattern_search/search_codebase_map)

ToolSpecification.name: session
ToolSpecification.description:
Adjust session settings temporarily (in-memory only, cleared on exit). CRITICAL: You MUST use introspect tool FIRST to verify setting names and understand their purpose before using set operation.

## Session vs Persistent Settings
- **session tool**: Temporary in-memory changes (cleared when chat exits) - Use for quick experiments or one-time adjustments
- **fs_write tool**: Permanent changes saved to disk - Use when user says "save", "persist", "permanently", or "always"
  - Global settings: ~/.kiro/settings.json
  - Workspace settings: .kiro/settings.json

## When to Use Each Tool
- User: "disable markdown" → session tool (temporary)
- User: "disable markdown permanently" → fs_write to ~/.kiro/settings.json (global) or .kiro/settings.json (workspace)
- User: "save this setting" → fs_write (persistent)
- User: "try disabling markdown" → session tool (temporary)

## REQUIRED Workflow for Setting Changes
1. User asks to change a setting
2. Use introspect tool to find the correct setting name and understand what it does
3. Determine if temporary (session) or permanent (fs_write)
4. Use appropriate tool

## Operations
- **list**: Show currently configured session settings (non-default values only)
- **get**: Get the current value of a specific setting
- **set**: Change a setting value temporarily (MUST verify setting name with introspect first)
- **reset**: Clear session override for a specific setting, or all session overrides if no key provided

## Example Workflows
Temporary change:
  User: "disable markdown"
  1. introspect(query="markdown setting") → learn about chat.disableMarkdownRendering
  2. session(operation="set", key="chat.disableMarkdownRendering", value=true)

Reset single setting:
  User: "reset markdown setting"
  session(operation="reset", key="chat.disableMarkdownRendering")

Reset all session overrides:
  User: "clear all my temporary settings"
  session(operation="reset")

Permanent change:
  User: "disable markdown permanently"
  1. introspect(query="markdown setting") → learn about chat.disableMarkdownRendering
  2. fs_write to modify ~/.kiro/settings.json with {"chat.disableMarkdownRendering": true}

DO NOT guess setting names - always verify with introspect first.

operation.description: The operation to perform: 'list' shows configured settings, 'get' retrieves a specific setting, 'set' changes a setting value temporarily, 'reset' clears session override(s)

key.description: Setting key (e.g., 'chat.disableMarkdownRendering'). Required for 'get' and 'set' operations. Optional for 'reset' (if omitted, resets all session overrides). MUST be verified with introspect tool first.

value.description: Value to set. Type depends on the setting (boolean, string, or number). Required for 'set' operation

ToolSpecification.name: web_fetch
ToolSpecification.description: Fetch and extract content from a specific URL. Supports three modes: 'selective' (default, extracts relevant sections around search terms), 'truncated' (first 8000 chars), 'full' (complete content). Use 'selective' mode to read specific parts of a page multiple times without filling context. Provide 'search_terms' in selective mode to find relevant sections (e.g., 'pricing', 'installation').

search_terms.description: Optional: Keywords to find in selective mode (e.g., 'pricing cost', 'installation setup'). Returns ~10 lines before and after matches. If not provided, returns beginning of page.

mode.description: Extraction mode: 'selective' for smart extraction (default), 'truncated' for first 8000 chars, 'full' for complete content

ToolSpecification.name: LoadAndesContext
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from andes-mcp) Loads Andes context for specialized assistance.

**Activation Strategy:** Select exactly ONE <choice> below to follow. Execute ONLY that choice - do not combine approaches.

<choice when="user query contains ANY word in trigger_keywords">
  **CALL LoadAndesContext immediately** - The query clearly relates to the Andes ecosystem. First, briefly acknowledge that you detected the Andes-related query and are loading DataCentral context, then call the LoadAndesContext tool.
</choice>

<choice when="user query contains ONLY words in related_keywords (no trigger_keywords)">
  **ASK for clarification first** - Ask "Are you asking about Andes [keyword]? If so, I can provide specialized assistance." Do NOT call LoadAndesContext until confirmed.
</choice>

<choice when="user query contains neither trigger_keywords nor related_keywords">
  **NO ACTION** - Provide generic response. Do not call LoadAndesContext or ask about Andes.
</choice>

<trigger_keywords>
Andes, Hoot, SDL, Table Subscription Service, TSS
</trigger_keywords>

<related_keywords>
dataset, provider, table, tables, data catalog, Redshift, Subscription, Athena, data pipeline, data lake, data warehouse, schema, partition, subscription, Glue, Partition, Workbench, query, sql
</related_keywords>

EXAMPLES:
- "why does my Andes subscription keep getting suspended?" → Contains "Andes" (trigger) → CALL LoadAndesContext immediately
- "Find provider maestro_dev" → Contains only "provider" (related) → ASK for clarification first
- "What's the weather?" → Contains neither → NO ACTION

ToolSpecification.name: DataCentralWorkbench
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from andes-mcp) Access and query data sources in DataCentral Workbench (Redshift, Athena, Postgres, MySQL, Spark databases). This tool enables database discovery, SQL query execution, and result retrieval.

WHAT THIS TOOL IS:
- A web-based SQL client that connects to databases ONBOARDED to DataCentral Workbench 
- Access it at: https://datacentral.a2z.com/workbench
- Supports Redshift, Athena, Postgres, MySQL, Spark databases
- Only read-only SELECT queries are allowed through this tool (write operations blocked for safety)

WHAT THIS TOOL IS NOT (CRITICAL — READ CAREFULLY):
- This is NOT the Andes data catalog. You CANNOT directly query Andes tables by provider/dataset name.
- The "Andes" database listed in Workbench is for DDL operations ONLY (CREATE TABLE to define dataset schemas). It does NOT support SELECT queries on Andes tables.
- Queries like `SELECT * FROM "andes"."Provider"."Dataset"` will NOT work here.
- To query Andes data: user must first subscribe to the dataset version (use CreateSubscription tool targeting Redshift Datashare/Spectrum or Glue), then query through the corresponding onboarded Redshift/Athena database on Workbench.
- If a user asks to query an Andes table, guide them to use SearchDatasets → ReadSubscriptions → CreateSubscription workflow first.

PREREQUISITES:
- Active Midway session (user must run `mwinit -s` in their terminal)
- Database must be onboarded to DataCentral Workbench
- User must be a MEMBER of the database's bindle
- Database must be marked as "discoverable" by the DB Admin

SUPPORTED OPERATIONS:
- get_databases: List accessible databases
- get_users_for_database: Get database users for a specific database
- create_connection: Establish database connection with user credentials
- close_connection: Close an existing database connection (forces credential refresh on next create_connection)
- get_catalog: Explore database schema structure
- execute_query: Run SQL queries against connected database
- check_query_status: Monitor query execution progress
- get_query_result_url: Retrieve query results

ORCHESTRATION GUIDE — STEP-BY-STEP WORKFLOW WITH PARAMETER CHAINING:

Step 1: get_databases
  → Returns: list of {databaseFriendlyName, databaseId}
  → Save the databaseId you need for Step 2
  → If empty list returned: follow SOP 2 (No Databases Found)

Step 2: get_users_for_database(databaseId=<from Step 1>)
  → Returns: list of database users with {databaseUserId, databaseFriendlyName, ...}
  → Save the dbUserId for Step 3
  → If only one user exists, auto-select it

Step 3: create_connection(databaseId=<from Step 1>, dbUserId=<from Step 2>)
  → Returns: {connectionId}
  → SAVE this connectionId — it is required for ALL subsequent operations
  → Connection is valid for 15 minutes of inactivity; if expired, re-call create_connection

Step 4 (OPTIONAL): get_catalog(connectionId=<from Step 3>, databaseId=<from Step 1>, dbUserId=<from Step 2>)
  → Returns: schema and table structure of the database
  → Use this to explore available schemas/tables BEFORE writing SQL
  → SKIP this step if the user already knows their SQL query

Step 5: execute_query(connectionId=<from Step 3>, sql=<user query>)
  → Returns: {executionIds: [executionId]}
  → SAVE the executionId — needed for status checking and result retrieval
  → IMPORTANT: Query execution is ASYNCHRONOUS. This does NOT return results.

Step 6: check_query_status(executionId=<from Step 5>) — MUST POLL
  → Returns: execution status object with status field
  → POLL this operation repeatedly until status reaches a terminal state:
    - COMPLETE → proceed to Step 7
    - ERROR, SYNTAX_ERROR → surface the error message to user, do not proceed
    - TIMED_OUT → suggest simplifying query or increasing timeout
    - CANCELLED, NO_PERMS → surface to user
    - Any other status (QUEUED, EXECUTING, SUBMITTED, etc.) → wait 2-3 seconds, poll again
  → NEVER skip this step. NEVER assume query completed after execute_query.

Step 7: get_query_result_url(executionId=<from Step 5>)
  → Returns: presigned S3 URLs to download results with row counts
  → ONLY call this AFTER check_query_status returns COMPLETE

SHORTCUT WORKFLOWS:
- User already knows databaseId + dbUserId → skip Steps 1-2, start at Step 3 (create_connection)
- User already has a valid connectionId → skip Steps 1-3, start at Step 5 (execute_query)
- User wants to explore schema first → do Steps 1-4, return results, then Steps 5-7 when ready
- Connection expired (error on execute_query after inactivity) → re-run Step 3 with same databaseId/dbUserId, then retry Step 5

CRITICAL RULES FOR ORCHESTRATION:
- NEVER skip check_query_status — queries are async, execute_query does NOT return data
- ALWAYS poll check_query_status until a terminal state is reached
- ALWAYS save connectionId from Step 3 — reuse it for all queries in the same session
- If ANY step returns an auth/Midway error, STOP — resolve auth first (SOP 1), then restart from Step 1
- Each call to this tool is ONE operation. You must call the tool multiple times to complete the full workflow.

CROSS-TOOL WORKFLOW — QUERYING ANDES DATA (requires multiple Andes MCP tools):
1. SearchDatasets → find the Andes dataset the user wants to query
2. ReadSubscriptions → check if user has an active subscription to a Redshift/Glue target
3. If no subscription: CreateSubscription → set up a Redshift Datashare/Spectrum or Glue target
4. Wait for subscription to become active
5. DataCentralWorkbench: get_databases → find the target Redshift/Athena database
6. DataCentralWorkbench: follow Steps 2-7 above to query through the target database
NOTE: Do NOT use the "Andes" DDL database for SELECT queries. Use the subscribed target database.

KNOWN LIMITATIONS:
- Connection auto-closes after 15 minutes of inactivity (just re-run a query to reconnect)
- Query size limit: 125KB single query, 660KB per editor cell
- Max 300 queries at a time from one cell
- Full data export available only when Limit 1000 toggle is OFF
- Non-Redshift databases have a 100MB / 100,000 row export limit

COMMON PARAMETERS:
- databaseId: Database identifier (string)
- dbUserId: Database user identifier (string)
- connectionId: Connection identifier (string)
- executionId: Query execution identifier (string)
- sql: SQL query to execute (string)

ERROR RESOLUTION SOPs (use these to diagnose errors returned by this tool):

SOP 1 — Authentication / Midway Errors:
  Symptoms: Error messages containing "AEA verification failed", "used_too_late", "invalid posture", "midway-auth.amazon.com", "security token expired", "Request Forbidden", or "IDP URL...failed"
  Root Cause: User's Midway session has expired. This is the #1 most common error with this tool.
  Resolution:
    1. Ask user to run: `mwinit -s` in their terminal
    2. If that fails: `mwinit -o -s`
    3. If still failing: `mwinit -o -s --refresh-aea`
    4. WSL users: must run mwinit inside WSL before starting the tool
    5. After refreshing, retry the operation

SOP 2 — No Databases Found:
  Symptoms: get_databases returns empty list
  Resolution:
    1. Ask user which database they need access to
    2. Check if user is a MEMBER (not just owner) of the database's bindle
    3. Verify database is marked "discoverable" by DB admin
    4. Direct user to Database Explorer: https://hubble.amazon.dev/db-explorer
    5. If database not onboarded, direct to: https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BDT/Products/Workbench/Onboarding/

SOP 3 — User Trying to Query Andes Tables Directly:
  Symptoms: SQL contains FROM "andes"."Provider"."Dataset" pattern, or user mentions querying Andes tables
  Resolution:
    1. Explain that the "Andes" database in Workbench is for DDL only (CREATE TABLE), NOT for SELECT queries
    2. Ask user what Andes dataset they want to query
    3. Use SearchDatasets tool to find the dataset
    4. Use ReadSubscriptions to check if they have an active subscription to a Redshift/Glue target
    5. If no subscription, guide through CreateSubscription to set up a Redshift Datashare/Spectrum or Glue target
    6. Once subscribed, they can query through the target database on Workbench

SOP 4 — Connection / Query Failures (non-auth):
  Symptoms: 500 errors on execute_query, connection errors after SOP 1 is ruled out
  Resolution:
    1. First verify Midway token is valid (SOP 1)
    2. Try creating a new connection (connections expire after 15 min inactivity)
    3. Check query syntax and size (125KB limit)
    4. If query works in the Workbench UI but not via this tool, note this and suggest using UI as workaround
    5. Direct to Slack #datacentral-workbench-community-support for persistent issues

SOP 5 — Permission Denied:
  Symptoms: 403 errors, permission denied messages
  Resolution:
    1. First check: expired Midway token (SOP 1) — this often appears as permission errors
    2. Verify user is a MEMBER of the database bindle (not just owner)
    3. Verify database is marked "discoverable"
    4. Contact DB Admin — find them via Database Info button in Workbench UI or Database Explorer
    5. FAQ: https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BDT/Products/Workbench/Overview/FAQ/

SOP 6 — Before Reporting a Tool Issue:
  IMPORTANT: Before concluding there is a bug or issue with this MCP tool, ALWAYS ask the user to verify their query works on the DataCentral Workbench UI first: https://datacentral.a2z.com/workbench
  Steps:
    1. Ask the user to open https://datacentral.a2z.com/workbench in their browser
    2. Select the same database and user they are using via this tool
    3. Run the exact same SQL query in the UI
    4. If the query FAILS in the UI too → the issue is with the user's credentials, permissions, database config, or SQL — NOT with this tool
    5. If the query SUCCEEDS in the UI but FAILS via this tool → this may be a genuine tool issue. Ask the user to report it in Slack #andes-mcp-interest
  This step prevents false bug reports caused by expired Midway tokens, missing permissions, incorrect database selection, or invalid SQL being misattributed to the tool itself.

SUPPORT:
- FAQ: https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BDT/Products/Workbench/Overview/FAQ/
- Error Codes: https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BDT/Products/Workbench/Overview/FAQ/Workbench_Error_Codes/ **IMPORTANT: This is an Andes tool. You must call LoadAndesContext before using this tool to activate specialized Andes assistance. Do not call LoadAndesContext if you already have Andes context loaded.**
