ToolSpecification.name: GenerateAndesSqlQuery
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from andes-mcp) Generates SQL queries for Andes tables by leveraging an intelligent agent with deep knowledge
of Andes schemas, business logic, and best practices.

**IMPORTANT: This tool calls an intelligent SQL generation agent, not a simple function.**

**Proxy Behavior:**
You must act as a proxy between the user and the SQL generation agent. When the SQL agent asks questions
or requests clarification, relay those questions directly to the user. Do NOT make assumptions or provide
default answers on behalf of the user.

**How to Use This Tool:**
- **Initial Call:** Provide comprehensive context including the user's query AND any relevant context from
  your conversation history (user preferences, regional rules, provider priorities, etc.). The SQL agent
  has no access to your conversation history, so include all relevant information in this initial call.
- **Session Management:** The SQL agent has conversational memory via sessionId. It remembers previous
  exchanges within the same session, so avoid repeating information already provided in that session.
  Always use the sessionId from previous responses when continuing a conversation.
- **Follow-up Calls:** Continue using the same sessionId when:
  • Answering the SQL agent's questions
  • Requesting modifications to the generated SQL
  • Providing additional context or constraints for the same query
  • Refining or iterating on the current SQL query
- **Starting New Sessions:** Omit the sessionId to start fresh when the user:
  • Explicitly asks to "create a new query" or "start over"
  • Switches to a completely different dataset or business question
  • Indicates they want to begin a new SQL generation task
- **Important:** If the SQL agent asks questions, DO NOT attempt to answer them yourself.
  Answers must come from: (1) the user's current request, (2) your conversation history if already provided,
  or (3) by asking the user for clarification. Never make assumptions to answer the SQL agent's questions.

**Response Processing:**
The SQL agent may return "thinking" text mixed with its final response. You MUST attempt to filter out
internal reasoning/thinking portions and present only the relevant answer, SQL query, and explanations
to the user. Look for clear transitions between thinking and final response content. **IMPORTANT: This is an Andes tool. You must call LoadAndesContext before using this tool to activate specialized Andes assistance. Do not call LoadAndesContext if you already have Andes context loaded.**

andes_mcp_user_context.description: The user's and AI agent's exact prompt(s) that led to this tool being called. Add any useful context if necessary. Include any useful parameters the tool is being called with or relevant parameters from previous tool calls. Use multiple sentences if needed.

sessionId.description: Session ID from previous response. Include to continue the same conversation, omit to start a new session

userQuery.description: The user's SQL request with full context for the SQL agent, including: query requirements, dataset/provider preferences, regional settings, time ranges, business constraints, and any relevant conversation history

dataMartArn.description: Optional ARN of an Andes dataMart to scope SQL generation to a curated business domain context (format: arn:amzn:andes:::dataMartVersion:{providerName}/{dataMartName}/{version}). A dataMart is a collection of curated datasets and business logic. Only one dataMart can be referenced at a time. Mutually exclusive with datasetArns — provide one or the other, not both.

dialect.description: SQL dialect for query generation. Pass only when the user explicitly mentions one of the supported dialects (Redshift, Spark SQL, or Presto). For any other dialect or if no dialect is mentioned, omit this parameter.

ToolSpecification.name: DebugCradleJobRunWFD
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from andes-mcp) Analyze completeness issues for Cradle job runs by examining dependencies, calculating intervals, and checking completeness status.

This tool performs a comprehensive 6-step analysis:
1. Validates API connectivity and required dependencies
2. Parses and validates the Cradle job run URL
3. Retrieves job run information including dataset date and Andes sources
4. Retrieves job configuration and dependency definitions
5. Analyzes each dependency's completeness status with proper partition mapping
6. Generates a formatted report with actionable insights and upstream job links

The tool provides detailed reports showing job status, dependency analysis, and direct links to upstream jobs for unsatisfied dependencies. **IMPORTANT: This is an Andes tool. You must call LoadAndesContext before using this tool to activate specialized Andes assistance. Do not call LoadAndesContext if you already have Andes context loaded.** **IMPORTANT: This is an Andes tool. ToolSpecification.name: retrieve_tacs_metrics
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from ecs-operator-mcp) 
    Retrieve TACS metrics from S3 for a specific task or container instance.

    This tool retrieves historical task metrics stored in S3 by the TACS (Telemetry Agent Communication Service).
    Metrics are stored in regional TACS accounts in 20-second aggregation intervals.

    Filter by task_arn to get metrics for a single task, or by container_instance_arn to get metrics for all
    tasks on a container instance. Both can be provided to narrow results further.

    For large time ranges, provide log_file_location to save results to a file instead of returning them inline.

    Args:
        tacs_host: TACS host name from connection logs
        region: AWS region
        start_time: Start time (ISO 8601 UTC)
        end_time: End time (ISO 8601 UTC)
        stage: Environment stage (prod, gamma)
        task_arn: Full ECS task ARN (optional)
        container_instance_arn: Full container instance ARN (optional)
        log_file_location: File path to save large results (optional)

    Returns:
        JSON string with metrics data and metadata

container_instance_arn.description: Full container instance ARN (filter by container instance). At least one of task_arn or container_instance_arn required.
container_instance_arn.title: Container Instance Arn

task_arn.title: Task Arn
task_arn.description: Full ECS task ARN (filter by task). At least one of task_arn or container_instance_arn required.

log_file_location.title: Log File Location
log_file_location.description: File path to save large results. If provided, metrics are saved to file and the path is returned instead of inline data.

ToolSpecification.name: falinks_run_query
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from ecs-operator-mcp) 
    Execute arbitrary SQL query against ECS Analytics.

    CRITICAL: ALWAYS CHECK TABLE SCHEMA FIRST
    Before constructing ANY query, you MUST check the database schema:
    1. Use falinks_get_table_schema for each table you plan to query
    2. Review ALL column names, types, and descriptions
    3. Use the EXACT column names from the schema (e.g., task_definition_account_id, NOT account_id)
    4. Construct your query using only verified column names

    This prevents query errors and ensures you use the correct column names.

    Database Selection Guide:
    * EVALUATE query time window - For queries with a window of less than one hour from current time,
    use the diff database. For queries looking at data older than 60 days from current time should
    use the snapshots database
    * EVALUATE status of resource - For queries on deleted or failed resources use the diff database.
    If the query looks at the lifecycle changes of a resource (e.g. create → update → update → delete)
    use the diff database.
    * USE diff database if the query is looking to gather customer impact for an event occuring in
    the last 1 week of current time or is looking to gather numbers of FAILED resouces.

    Diff Database Usage Guide:
    * MUST USE the txn_time field to define over what time period the query should run, such as
    WHERE txn_time BETWEEN timestamp '2023-03-08 10:00' AND timestamp '2023-03-08 10:30'
    * USE the DISTINCT filter on records IF the query is looking to gather absolute impact numbers
    as the diff database contains all record state changes.

    Snapshots Database Usage Guide:
    * USE the TIMESTAMP for rows with age > 60 days or for queries that need to run over the whole
    table such as SELECT * FROM tasks FOR TIMESTAMP AS OF TIMESTAMP '2022-12-16 00:00:00 UTC' WHERE id ='<task id>'

    Parameters:
    -----------
    query: str
    SQL query string to execute
    database: str
    Database name (snapshots or diff)
    stage: str
    The stage: beta, gamma, zeta, prod
    region: str
    The AWS region or airport code

    Returns:
    --------
    JSON string with query results.

stage.description: The stage: beta, gamma, zeta, prod
stage.title: Stage

log_file_location.title: Log File Location
log_file_location.description: Folder name (usually ticket ID) for storing results. If provided, raw logs are uploaded to S3; otherwise, saved in local temp folder.

region.title: Region
region.description: The AWS region or airport code

ToolSpecification.name: heimdall_trace_request
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from ecs-operator-mcp) 
    Trace requests using Heimdall service to diagnose customer request failures across AWS services.

    Diagnose customer request failures by tracing request IDs across AWS services.

    VALIDATION: Either provide 'url' (a Heimdall URL containing '/requests/'), OR provide both
    'request_id' and 'region'. Providing only one of 'request_id'/'region' without the other
    (and without 'url') will result in a validation error.

    Args:
        request_id: The request ID to trace. Required if 'url' is not provided.
        region: AWS region to search in. Required if 'url' is not provided.
        url: Full Heimdall URL containing '/requests/'. If provided, request_id and region are optional.

ToolSpecification.name: batchgetcoeanalyses
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from coe-mcp) Retrieve detailed information for multiple COE (Correction of Error) analyses by their analysis IDs.

## PROCESSING COE ANALYSES:
This tool fetches complete COE analysis data including full content text, metadata, and workflow information.

**INPUT REQUIREMENTS:**
- Provide analysis IDs (UUIDs) - NOT reference IDs like "COE-2025-001"
- Maximum limits: 20 IDs for /dev-mcp endpoint, 5 IDs for regular endpoints
- IDs can be obtained from search-coe-analyses results

**RETURNED DATA FOR EACH ANALYSIS:**
- **Complete Analysis Content**: Full text content of the COE analysis
- **Metadata**: Title, reference ID, publication state, incident details
- **Workflow Information**: Author, owner, creation/published/due dates
- **Flags**: Team internal, rework status, open action items

## WORKFLOW FOR PROCESSING LARGE COE SETS:
1. **From Search Results**: Use analysis IDs from search-coe-analyses (especially from the 101+ ID-only list)
2. **Batch Processing**: Process large sets in chunks respecting the endpoint limits
3. **Prioritization**: Start with most recent or most relevant analyses from search results
4. **Content Analysis**: Use the full text content for detailed analysis, pattern recognition, or summarization

AnalysisIds.description: List of COE analysis IDs to retrieve (max 20)

ToolSpecification.name: search_action_items
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from coe-mcp) Search COE action items with structured filters including owner, org hierarchy, due dates, priorities, and status.

## COMMON QUERY PATTERNS:
- **Action items in someone's org**: Use OwnerParent with the manager's alias (e.g. "robertke")
- **Action items owned by a specific person**: Use Owner with their alias (e.g. "johndoe")
- **Action items for a specific COE**: Use SourceDocumentId with the COE ID or UUID
- **Overdue items**: Use HasStatus="OPEN" with DueBefore set to today's date

## STRUCTURED ATTRIBUTES:
ALWAYS use structured fields. For status filtering use HasStatus, for priority use IncludedPriorities, for org-level queries use OwnerParent, for individual lookup use Owner.

CreatedBefore.description: Action items created before this date

OwnerParent.description: Manager alias to search within their org hierarchy — returns all action items under this manager

LastUpdatedBefore.description: Action items last updated before this date

LastUpdatedAfter.description: Action items last updated after this date

DueBefore.description: Action items due before this date (YYYY-MM-DD or ISO datetime)

AnalysisId.description: Filter by linked COE analysis ID

DueAfter.description: Action items due after this date (YYYY-MM-DD or ISO datetime)

CreatedAfter.description: Action items created after this date

SourceDocumentId.description: COE identifier — accepts both short reference IDs (e.g. "274719") and full UUIDs

ToolSpecification.name: searchcoeanalyses
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from coe-mcp) Search through COE (Correction of Error) analyses and post-incident reports documenting system failures, root causes, and corrective actions using standard search parameters.

## PAGINATION WITH NEXTTOKEN:
**CRITICAL**: When a NextToken is provided in the response, ALWAYS ask the user if they want to fetch more results.

**Usage Instructions**:
- **Initial search**: Leave NextToken empty to get first page of results
- **User requests more results**: Use the SAME search parameters from the previous query, but add the NextToken from the previous response
- **IMPORTANT**: When user asks for "next page" or "more results", reuse ALL the original search criteria (TextKeywordSearch, Labels, dates, etc.) and ONLY add the NextToken parameter
- **Response Format**: When more results are available, the response will show "Found X COE result(s) (more results available)"
- **When NextToken is provided**: Always ask "Would you like me to fetch the next page of results using the NextToken?"

## IMPORTANT WORKFLOW GUIDANCE:
This tool fetches metadata for COE analyses with optimized result display to manage large result sets effectively:

**RESULT DISPLAY FORMAT:**
- **First 100 COEs**: Full metadata details including title, reference ID, incident date, author, publication state, flags, and COE links
- **COEs 101+**: Only analysis IDs are shown (no metadata details to prevent context overflow)
- All results are sorted by incident date (most recent first)

**WORKING WITH RESULTS:**
1. **Small result sets (≤100 COEs)**: All analyses show complete metadata - review and use as needed

2. **Large result sets (100+ COEs)**: 
   - The first 100 COEs contain full metadata for initial review
   - COEs beyond 100 show only their analysis IDs
   - Use batch-get-coe-analyses tool with specific analysis IDs from the 101+ list to get full details for additional COEs of interest
   - Batch limits: max 20 IDs for /dev-mcp endpoint, max 5 IDs for regular endpoints

3. **Best practices for large searches**:
   - Review the first 100 detailed results to identify patterns and relevance
   - Refine your search with more specific filters (dates, labels, keywords) to reduce result size
   - Extract specific analysis IDs from the 101+ list for targeted follow-up using batch-get-coe-analyses
   - Use NextToken to paginate through additional results when provided

## KEYWORD EXPANSION:
If a user searches for a specific topic, expand the user query into multiple related terms in TextKeywordSearch. For example, convert general topics into those seen below:
- 'DynamoDB issues' → ['dynamodb', 'ddb', 'dynamodb throttling', 'partition key', 'hot partition']
- 'Lambda problems' → ['lambda timeout', 'lambda failure', 'serverless function', 'invocation error']
- 'AZ failures' → ['az', 'availability zone', 'az failure', 'az impairment', 'zone isolation', 'multi-az']
- 'Network issues' → ['network', 'connectivity', 'dns', 'routing', 'vpc', 'subnet']

Use specific technical terms, service names, and error types instead of generic words like 'failure' or 'issue'.

## LABELS: CRITICAL 
- Only use labels that exist in the predefined enum list. For human/operator errors use 'Human Error' and 'Operator Error' (exact capitalization). For configuration issues use 'Configuration Issue'. 

Never create custom labels - only select from the exact enum values provided in the schema.

## USER ENTITIES:
For user-related parameters (Author, Owner, etc.), use correct object format: {"Type": "LDAP_USER", "Id": "useralias"} for users or {"Type": "LDAP_GROUP", "Id": "teamname"} for teams.

## STRUCTURED ATTRIBUTES:
ALWAYS prioritize structured schema fields over TextKeywordSearch. Use TextKeywordSearch ONLY if the information cannot be captured by any other field (Labels, Flags, dates, user objects, etc.). 

For 'open action items' use Flags.ActionItemOpen=true, for publication status use PublicationState, for dates use specific date fields, for user filtering use Author/Owner objects. 

TextKeywordSearch is a last resort for truly unstructured content that has no corresponding schema field.

AuthorParent.description: Parent team or organization of the COE author

PublishedAfter.description: Filter by when the COE was officially published/approved

Editors.description: People with edit permissions on the COE

ToolSpecification.name: aws___read_documentation
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) Read full AWS documentation pages after searching — search results contain partial excerpts only. Use this tool on the URLs returned by `search_documentation` to get complete, accurate information.

## Usage

This tool reads documentation pages concurrently and converts them to markdown format.
Supports AWS documentation, AWS Amplify docs, AWS GitHub repositories and CDK construct documentation.
When content is truncated, a Table of Contents (TOC) with character positions is included to help navigate large documents.

## Best Practices

- After searching, read the most relevant URLs to get complete information — search snippets are partial excerpts and often insufficient to answer accurately
- Batch 2-5 requests when reading multiple URLs from search results
- Use TOC character positions to jump directly to relevant sections in long documents
- If a document was truncated and the answer may be in the remaining content, continue reading with `start_index` set to the previous `end_index`. Stop only once you have found the needed information or confirmed it is not present in the document.

## Request Format

Each request must be an object with:
- `url`: The documentation URL to fetch (required)
- `max_length`: Maximum characters to return (optional, default: 10000 characters)
- `start_index`: Starting character position (optional, default: 0)

For batching you can input a list of requests.

## Example Request

```
{
  "requests":
  [
      {
          "url": "https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-management.html",
          "max_length": 5000,
          "start_index": 0
      },
      {
          "url": "https://repost.aws/knowledge-center/ec2-instance-connection-troubleshooting"
      }
  ]
}
```


## URL Requirements

Allow-listed URL prefixes:
- docs.aws.amazon.com
- aws.amazon.com
- repost.aws/knowledge-center
- docs.amplify.aws
- ui.docs.amplify.aws
- github.com/aws-cloudformation/aws-cloudformation-templates
- github.com/aws-samples/aws-cdk-examples
- github.com/aws-samples/generative-ai-cdk-constructs-samples
- github.com/aws-samples/serverless-patterns
- github.com/awsdocs/aws-cdk-guide
- github.com/awslabs/aws-solutions-constructs
- github.com/cdklabs/cdk-nag
- constructs.dev/packages/@aws-cdk-containers
- constructs.dev/packages/@aws-cdk
- constructs.dev/packages/@cdk-cloudformation
- constructs.dev/packages/aws-analytics-reference-architecture
- constructs.dev/packages/aws-cdk-lib
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk-amazon-chime-resources
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk-aws-lambda-powertools-layer
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk-ecr-deployment
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk-lambda-powertools-python-layer
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk-serverless-clamscan
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk8s
- constructs.dev/packages/cdk8s-plus-33
- strandsagents.com/

Deny-listed URL prefixes:
- aws.amazon.com/marketplace

## Example URLs

- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/bucketnamingrules.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-invocation.html
- https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2023/02/aws-telco-network-builder/

- https://ui.docs.amplify.aws/angular/connected-components/authenticator
- https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-cdk-examples/blob/main/README.md
- https://github.com/awslabs/aws-solutions-constructs/blob/main/README.md
- https://constructs.dev/packages/aws-cdk-lib/v/2.229.1?submodule=aws_lambda&lang=typescript
- https://github.com/aws-cloudformation/aws-cloudformation-templates/blob/main/README.md
- https://strandsagents.com/docs/user-guide/quickstart/overview/index.md

## Output Format

Returns a list of results, one per request:
- Success: Markdown content with `status: "SUCCESS"`, `total_length`, `start_index`, `end_index`, `truncated`, `redirected_url` (if page was redirected)
- Error: Error message with `status: "ERROR"`, `error_code` (not_found, invalid_url, throttled, downstream_error, validation_error)
- Truncated content includes a ToC with character positions for navigation
- Redirected pages include a note in the content and populate the `redirected_url` field

## Handling Long Documents

If the response indicates the document was truncated, you have several options:

1. **Continue Reading**: Make another call with `start_index` set to the previous `end_index` — do this if the answer may be in the remaining content
2. **Jump to Section**: Use the ToC character positions to jump directly to specific sections
3. **Stop when done**: Stop only once you have found the needed information or confirmed it is not present in the document

**Example - Jump to Section:**
```
# TOC shows: "Using a logging library (char 3331-6016)"
# Jump directly to that section:
{"requests":[{"url": "https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/python-logging.html", "start_index": 3331, "max_length": 3000}]}
```

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more content is required.

url.description: URL of the AWS documentation page to read.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.

requests.description: List of documentation requests, each containing url, max_length (optional), and start_index (optional).

ToolSpecification.name: aws___search_documentation
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) # AWS Documentation Search Tool
Use this tool to find relevant AWS documentation — always follow up with `read_documentation` to get complete answers. Prefer this over general knowledge for AWS services, features, configurations, troubleshooting, and best practices.

## When to Use This Tool

**Always search when the query involves:**
- Any AWS service or feature (Lambda, S3, EC2, RDS, etc.)
- AWS architecture, patterns, or best practices
- AWS CLI, SDK, or API usage
- AWS CDK or CloudFormation
- AWS Amplify development
- AWS errors or troubleshooting
- AWS pricing, limits, or quotas
- Strands Agents development
- "How do I..." questions about AWS
- Recent AWS updates or announcements

**Only skip this tool when:**
- Query is about non-AWS technologies
- Question is purely conceptual (e.g., "What is a database?")
- General programming questions unrelated to AWS

## Skill Suggestions for Actionable Queries

When your search query matches tasks that benefit from domain-specific expertise, this tool will suggest relevant **Agent Skills**. Skills package domain knowledge, workflows, best practices, decision frameworks, and reference materials that make you a specialist in a particular AWS domain.

**How it works:**
- Your search query is scored against the skills registry using semantic search over skill descriptions and metadata tags
- If your query matches a skill's domain, relevant skills are returned alongside documentation results
- Skills cover a wide range of domains: deployment, troubleshooting, security, optimization, architecture, and more
- To load a suggested skill, use the `retrieve_skill` tool with the `skill_name`
- Once loaded, follow the skill's workflows and retrieve any referenced files as needed

**Example queries that may return skills:**
- "deploy a web application to AWS" — may return a deployment skill with architecture guidance and step-by-step deployment instructions
- "debug Lambda cold start issues" — may return a troubleshooting skill with diagnostic workflows
- "secure S3 buckets" — may return a security skill with best practices and compliance checklists
- "optimize API Gateway latency" — may return a performance skill with decision frameworks
- "set up VPC peering" — may return a networking skill with step-by-step procedures

## Quick Topic Selection

| Query Type | Use Topic | Example |
|------------|-----------|-------|
| API/SDK/CLI code | `reference_documentation` | "S3 PutObject boto3", "Lambda invoke API" |
| New features, releases | `current_awareness` | "Lambda new features 2024", "what's new in ECS" |
| Errors, debugging | `troubleshooting` | "AccessDenied S3", "Lambda timeout error" |
| Amplify apps | `amplify_docs` | "Amplify Auth React", "Amplify Storage Flutter" |
| CDK concepts, APIs, CLI | `cdk_docs` | "CDK stack props Python", "cdk deploy command" |
| CDK code samples, patterns | `cdk_constructs` | "serverless API CDK", "Lambda function example TypeScript" |
| CloudFormation templates | `cloudformation` | "DynamoDB CloudFormation", "StackSets template" |
| Architecture, blogs, guides | `general` | "Lambda best practices", "S3 architecture patterns" |
| Strands Agents | `strands_docs` | "Strands Agents Python structured output", "Strands Agents AWS CDK EC2 Deployment Example" |
| Domain expertise, workflows, guided procedures | `agent_skills` | "deploy serverless app", "debug Lambda cold starts", "secure IAM policies" |

## Documentation Topics

### reference_documentation
**For: API methods, SDK code, CLI commands, technical specifications**

Use for:
- SDK method signatures: "boto3 S3 upload_file parameters"
- CLI commands: "aws ec2 describe-instances syntax"
- API references: "Lambda InvokeFunction API"
- Service configuration: "RDS parameter groups"

Don't confuse with general—use this for specific technical implementation.

### current_awareness
**For: New features, announcements, "what's new", release dates**

Use for:
- "New Lambda features"
- "When was EventBridge Scheduler released"
- "Latest S3 updates"
- "Is feature X available yet"

Keywords: new, recent, latest, announced, released, launch, available

### troubleshooting
**For: Error messages, debugging, problems, "not working"**

Use for:
- Error codes: "InvalidParameterValue", "AccessDenied"
- Problems: "Lambda function timing out"
- Debug scenarios: "S3 bucket policy not working"
- "How to fix..." queries

Keywords: error, failed, issue, problem, not working, how to fix, how to resolve

### amplify_docs
**For: Frontend/mobile apps with Amplify framework**

Always include framework: React, Next.js, Angular, Vue, JavaScript, React Native, Flutter, Android, Swift

Examples:
- "Amplify authentication React"
- "Amplify GraphQL API Next.js"
- "Amplify Storage Flutter setup"

### cdk_docs
**For: CDK concepts, API references, CLI commands, getting started**

Use for CDK questions like:
- "How to get started with CDK"
- "CDK stack construct TypeScript"
- "cdk deploy command options"
- "CDK best practices Python"
- "What are CDK constructs"

Include language: Python, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go

**Common mistake**: Using general knowledge instead of searching for CDK concepts and guides. Always search for CDK questions!

### cdk_constructs
**For: CDK code examples, patterns, L3 constructs, sample implementations**

Use for:
- Working code: "Lambda function CDK Python example"
- Patterns: "API Gateway Lambda CDK pattern"
- Sample apps: "Serverless application CDK TypeScript"
- L3 constructs: "ECS service construct"

Include language: Python, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go

### cloudformation
**For: CloudFormation templates, concepts, SAM patterns**

Use for:
- "CloudFormation StackSets"
- "DynamoDB table template"
- "SAM API Gateway Lambda"
- "CloudFormation template examples"

### strands_docs
**For: Strands Agents API reference, integrations, model providers, session managers, tools, examples, user-guide**

Use for:
- "Strands Agents Python SDK example"
- "Strands Agents AWS integration"
- "Strands Agents community contributions"
- "Strands Agents usage examples"
- "Strands Agents usage guide"

### general
**For: Architecture, best practices, tutorials, blog posts, design patterns**

Use for:
- Architecture patterns: "Serverless architecture AWS"
- Best practices: "S3 security best practices"
- Design guidance: "Multi-region architecture"
- Getting started: "Building data lakes on AWS"
- Tutorials and blog posts

**Common mistake**: Not using this for AWS conceptual and architectural questions. Always search for AWS best practices and patterns!

**Don't use general knowledge for AWS topics—search instead!**

### agent_skills
**For: Discovering agent skills — domain-specific expertise packages for AWS workflows**

Use for:
- Complex tasks that benefit from guided workflows: "deploy a serverless application"
- Troubleshooting scenarios: "debug Lambda cold starts", "resolve ECS task failures"
- Security and compliance: "secure S3 buckets", "review IAM policies for least privilege"
- Architecture and optimization: "optimize API Gateway latency", "design multi-region architecture"
- When you need domain expertise beyond what documentation provides

Skills go beyond documentation — they provide workflows, decision frameworks, best practices, and may include embedded procedures for critical sub-tasks.

**Important**: This topic is meant for discovery. Once you identify the skill you need, use `retrieve_skill` tool with the `skill_name` to load the full skill and its reference materials.

**Note**: If combined with other topics, skills will be mixed into the documentation results. Use `agent_skills` alone for a clean skill-only listing.

## Search Best Practices

**Be specific with service names:**

Good examples:
```
"S3 bucket versioning configuration"
"Lambda environment variables Python SDK"
"DynamoDB GSI query patterns"
```

Bad examples:
```
"versioning" (too vague)
"environment variables" (missing context)
```

**Include framework/language:**
```
"Amplify authentication React"
"CDK Lambda function TypeScript"
"boto3 S3 client Python"
```

**Use exact error messages:**
```
"AccessDenied error S3 GetObject"
"InvalidParameterValue Lambda environment"
```

**Add temporal context for new features:**
```
"Lambda new features 2024"
"recent S3 announcements"
```

**If the first search does not return results that directly answer the question, refine your query and search again with different terms, a more specific phrase, or a different topic. Try conceptual/architectural topics (general, blogs) if reference docs are too narrow.**

**After searching, use `read_documentation` on the top-ranked URLs to verify and complete your answer.**

## Multiple Topic Selection

You can search multiple topics simultaneously for comprehensive results:
```
# For a query about Lambda errors and new features:
topics=["troubleshooting", "current_awareness"]

# For CDK examples and API reference:
topics=["cdk_constructs", "cdk_docs"]

# For Amplify and general AWS architecture:
topics=["amplify_docs", "general"]

# For actionable tasks:
topics=["agent_skills"]
```

## Response Format

Results include:
- `rank_order`: Relevance score (lower = more relevant)
- `url`: Direct documentation link — use with `read_documentation` to get the full page content
- `title`: Page title
- `context`: Partial excerpt only — not the complete documentation. After reviewing results, call `read_documentation` on the most relevant URLs before answering. Do not answer based on the context excerpt alone.

## Parameters
```
search_phrase: str         # Required - your search query
topics: List[str]          # Optional - up to 3 topics. Defaults to ["general"]
limit: int = 5             # Optional - max results per topic
```

---

**Remember: When in doubt about AWS, always search. This tool provides the most current, accurate AWS information. But search is only step 1 — always read the full documentation to give complete answers.**

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return

topics.description: List of documentation topics to search. Available topics: reference_documentation, current_awareness, troubleshooting, amplify_docs, cdk_docs, cdk_constructs, cloudformation, agent_skills, strands_docs, general. Can specify multiple topics, up-to 3, to search across them. Use 'general' only if query doesn't match other topics.

ToolSpecification.name: aws___retrieve_skill
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) Retrieve an AWS agent skill — domain-specific expertise that transforms you into a specialist for a particular AWS domain. Skills provide workflows, context, best practices, decision frameworks and step-by-step procedures. A skill may include reference files (architecture docs, schemas, examples) and deterministic workflows for sub-tasks that require exact execution.

## What Skills Provide

- **Domain expertise**: Deep knowledge about specific AWS services, patterns, and operational practices
- **Workflows**: Guided sequences for complex tasks with appropriate degrees of freedom
- **Reference materials**: Architecture docs, API references, examples, and templates accessible via the `file` parameter
- **Decision frameworks**: Conditional logic and troubleshooting trees for navigating complex scenarios

## CRITICAL PREREQUISITE — DO NOT SKIP

You MUST call search_documentation BEFORE calling this tool. NEVER call this tool first. You do NOT know skill names — they are unpredictable identifiers that can only be discovered through search_documentation results. Guessing or fabricating a skill_name WILL fail.

## REQUIRED WORKFLOW (no exceptions)

1. FIRST: Call search_documentation with the user's requirements
2. THEN: Find the result entry that has a skill_name field
3. FINALLY: Call this tool with the EXACT skill_name value from that result — copy it verbatim

## Working with Skills

When you retrieve a skill:
1. Read the SKILL.md overview to understand the domain and scope
2. Follow the workflows and guidance in the skill body
3. When the skill references additional files (e.g., `[architecture](references/architecture.md)`), retrieve them using this same tool with the `file` parameter
4. Apply the skill's decision frameworks and conditional logic to the user's specific situation

## PARAMETER REQUIREMENTS

skill_name: str (Required)
- MUST be copied exactly from the skill_name field in search_documentation results
- Do NOT guess, fabricate, paraphrase, or modify the name in any way
- Do NOT use the result title — use only the skill_name field value

file: str (Optional)
- Retrieve a specific file within the skill directory (e.g., "references/architecture.md")
- Use this when the SKILL.md body links to reference files
- If omitted, returns the main SKILL.md file

## IF SKILL NOT FOUND

If you get an error, you likely guessed the name. Call search_documentation first to discover it. The error response will include a list of available files for the skill.

## Returns

The skill content — either the main SKILL.md with domain expertise, workflows, and guidance, or a specific reference file when the `file` parameter is provided.

file.description: Optional specific file within the skill directory (e.g., 'references/architecture.md'). Use when the SKILL.md body links to reference files. If omitted, returns the main SKILL.md.

skill_name.description: Exact skill name from the skill_name field in search_documentation results (no modifications)

ToolSpecification.name: aws___recommend
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) Get content recommendations for an AWS documentation page.

## Usage

This tool provides recommendations for related AWS documentation pages based on a given URL.
Use it to discover additional relevant content that might not appear in search results.
URL must be from the docs.aws.amazon.com domain.

## Recommendation Types

The recommendations include four categories:

1. **Highly Rated**: Popular pages within the same AWS service
2. **New**: Recently added pages within the same AWS service - useful for finding newly released features
3. **Similar**: Pages covering similar topics to the current page
4. **Journey**: Pages commonly viewed next by other users

## When to Use

- After reading a documentation page to find related content
- When exploring a new AWS service to discover important pages
- To find alternative explanations of complex concepts
- To discover the most popular pages for a service
- To find newly released information by using a service's welcome page URL and checking the **New** recommendations

## Finding New Features

To find newly released information about a service:
1. Find any page belong to that service, typically you can try the welcome page
2. Call this tool with that URL
3. Look specifically at the **New** recommendation type in the results

## Result Interpretation

Each recommendation includes:
- url: The documentation page URL
- title: The page title
- context: A brief description (if available)

url.description: URL of the AWS documentation page to get recommendations for

ToolSpecification.name: aws___list_regions
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) Retrieve a list of all AWS regions.

## Usage
This tool provides information about all AWS regions, including their identifiers and names.

## When to Use
- When planning global infrastructure deployments
- To validate region codes for other API calls
- To get a complete AWS regional inventory

## Do Not Use This Tool For
- Answering questions about how many regions exist in a geography (e.g., "how many AP regions?") — use this tool to get the full list, then count from the result, or use `search_documentation` for a documented answer
- Questions about service or feature availability in specific regions — use `get_regional_availability` for known product names, or `search_documentation` for general coverage questions
- Any question that can be answered from AWS documentation — use `search_documentation` instead

## Result Interpretation
Each region result includes:
- region_id: The unique region code (e.g., 'us-east-1') 
- region_long_name: The human-friendly name (e.g., 'US East (N. Virginia)')
 
## Common Use Cases
1. Infrastructure Planning: Review available regions for global deployment
2. Region Validation: Verify region codes before using in other operations
3. Regional Inventory: Get a complete list of AWS's global infrastructure

ToolSpecification.name: aws___get_regional_availability
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-knowledge-mcp-server-mcp) Check AWS resource availability across regions for products (service and features), APIs, and CloudFormation resources.

## Quick Reference
- Maximum 10 regions per call (split into multiple calls for more regions)
- Single region: filters optional, supports pagination
- Multiple regions: filters required, no pagination, queries run concurrently
- Status values: 'isAvailableIn' | 'isNotAvailableIn' | 'isPlannedIn' | 'Not Found'
- Response field: 'products' (product), 'service_apis' (api), 'cfn_resources' (cfn)

## When to Use
1. Pre-deployment Validation
    - Verify resource availability before deployment
    - Prevent deployment failures due to regional restrictions
    - Validate multi-region architecture requirements

2. Architecture Planning
    - Design region-specific solutions
    - Plan multi-region deployments
    - Compare regional capabilities

## Do Not Use This Tool For
- Counting or listing regions by geography (e.g., "how many AP regions exist?") — use `list_regions` then count, or use `search_documentation`
- Questions about documentation, announcements, or general service availability dates — use `search_documentation`
- CloudFormation resource coverage questions across all regions — use `search_documentation` with topic `cloudformation`
- Any question that asks about availability in general without specifying a known product name, API, or CFN resource type — use `search_documentation` instead, as this tool requires exact resource identifiers and will return 'Not Found' for vague queries

## Examples

**Check specific resources in one region**:
```
regions=["us-east-1"], resource_type="product", filters=["AWS Lambda"]
regions=["us-east-1"], resource_type="api", filters=["Lambda+Invoke", "S3+GetObject"]
regions=["us-east-1"], resource_type="cfn", filters=["AWS::Lambda::Function"]
```

**Compare availability across regions**:
```
regions=["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"], resource_type="product", filters=["AWS Lambda"]
```

**Explore all resources** (single region only, with pagination handling support via next_token due to large output):
```
regions=["us-east-1"], resource_type="product"
```
Follow up with next_token from response to get more results.

## Response Format

**Single Region**: Flat structure with optional next_token. Example:
```
{"products": {"AWS Lambda": "isAvailableIn"}, "next_token": null, "failed_regions": null}
```

**Multiple Regions**: Nested by region. Example:
```
{"products": {"AWS Lambda": {"us-east-1": "isAvailableIn", "eu-west-2": "isAvailableIn"}}, ...}
```

## Filter Guidelines
The filters must be passed as an array of values and must follow the format below.
1. Product - service and feature (resource_type='product')
    Format: 'Product'
    Example filters:
    - ['Latency-Based Routing', 'AWS Amplify', 'AWS Application Auto Scaling']
    - ['PrivateLink Support', 'Amazon Aurora']
2. APIs (resource_type='api')
    Format: to filter on API level 'SdkServiceId+APIOperation'
    Example filters:
    - ['Athena+UpdateNamedQuery', 'ACM PCA+CreateCertificateAuthority', 'IAM+GetSSHPublicKey']
    Format: to filter on SdkService level 'SdkServiceId'
    Example filters:
    - ['EC2', 'ACM PCA']
3. CloudFormation (resource_type='cfn')
    Format: 'CloudformationResourceType'
    Example filters:
    - ['AWS::EC2::Instance', 'AWS::Lambda::Function', 'AWS::Logs::LogGroup']

next_token.description: Pagination token from previous response for retrieving additional results. Only valid for single region queries and no filters.

regions.description: One or more AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1). Maximum 10 regions per call. Single region supports pagination. Multiple regions require filters.

resource_type.description: Type of AWS resource: 'product' (AWS services/features), 'api' (SDK/API operations), or 'cfn' (CloudFormation resource types).

filters.description:
Optional list of one or multiple specific resources to check. Format depends on resource_type:
- Products: ['AWS Lambda', 'Amazon S3']
- APIs: ['IAM+GetSSHPublicKey', 'EC2']
- CloudFormation: ['AWS::EC2::Instance']
Must follow the format specified in the tool description.

region.description: Target AWS region code (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-2).

ToolSpecification.name: searchspecartifacts
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Search across the analysis results and code graph of a spec for a given package revision.
Searches file paths in processedFiles and summary text in resultSummaries within the analysis/file-index.json artifact, and searches code-graph.md by filtering its markdown sections (delimited by headings) using case-insensitive partial keyword matching.
A keyword is required to filter results.
Note: resultSummaries only contains the title of each analysis result. To read the full analysis content, use the get-spec-content tool with the appropriate artifactPath as a follow-up.

keyword.description: Search keyword for case-insensitive partial matching

packageName.description: Package name to search artifacts for

revisionId.description: Revision ID. Uses latest valid if not provided

ToolSpecification.name: getrevisionmetadata
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve full revision metadata by name and revision ID.
For packages: Returns spec type, status, feature files, git commit info, and analyzer metadata.
For collections: Returns status, progress, creator, timestamps, errors, and available documents.
Use this when you have a specific revision ID and need detailed information about that revision.

generationType.description: Type of generated spec: PACKAGE or COLLECTION (default: PACKAGE)

ToolSpecification.name: set_user_setting
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Updates a specific user setting in the config file.

Supported settings:
- defaultDocPath: Default directory path for saving generated documentation (must be a valid directory)

Creates the config directory (~/.spec-studio-mcp/) and file if they don't exist.
Merges with existing settings (doesn't overwrite entire file).

key.description: The setting key to update. Valid keys: defaultDocPath

value.description: The new value for the setting

ToolSpecification.name: getcollectionmetadata
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve collection metadata by collection ID.
Returns collection details including name, description, creator, timestamps, linked elements, and most recent revision information with available documents.
Use this when you need information about a specific collection.

collectionId.description: The ID of the collection to retrieve

ToolSpecification.name: getallpackages
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve and browse specification packages from Spec Studio with pagination, search, and sorting.
Supports case-insensitive partial name matching with intelligent suggestions when no exact matches found.
Returns package information including ID, name, description, creator, branch, creation date, and target package count.
Use for package discovery before retrieving specific metadata or content.

search.description: Optional search term to filter packages by name (case-insensitive)

nextToken.description: Continuation token from previous response for pagination

ToolSpecification.name: queuegeneration
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Queue a package or collection for spec generation.
For packages: requires the package name and the git branch.
For collections: requires the collection ID (branch is ignored; latest successful revisions of linked elements are used).

IMPORTANT: Do NOT automatically queue generation. Always ask the user for confirmation before calling this tool.

branch.description: Git branch (e.g. "mainline"). Required for PACKAGE, ignored for COLLECTION.

generationType.description: Type of generation: PACKAGE or COLLECTION (default: PACKAGE)

ToolSpecification.name: semantic_search
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Search the Spec Studio knowledge base using natural language queries to find relevant documentation content.

Supports two search modes:
- Global search: Search across all packages in the knowledge base. Only requires queryText.
- Scoped search: Narrow results using the optional filters parameter:
  - packageFilters: Array of package scope filters (max 5). Each can specify packageName, branch, and/or revisionId.
  - collectionFilters: Array of collection scope filters (max 5). Each specifies collectionRevisionId.
  - Filters within each array are OR'd together, and results from both KBs are merged and sorted by score.

Query tips:
- Use specific keywords rather than full questions for better results (e.g., "accessibility validator" vs "What validators are supported?")
- More focused queries typically yield higher relevance scores and more precise matches

Each result includes the content excerpt, relevance score, and source location (packageId, packageName, branch, revisionId, originalUri).
Results are ranked by relevance to help find the most pertinent documentation.

collectionFilters.description: Array of collection filters (max 5). Filters are OR'd together.

collectionRevisionId.description: Filter results by collection revision ID

packageFilters.description: Array of package filters (max 5). Filters are OR'd together.

branch.description: Filter results to a specific branch

revisionId.description: Filter results to a specific revision ID

packageName.description: Filter results to a specific package name

filters.description: Optional filters to scope the search to specific packages and/or collections

numberOfResults.description: Maximum number of results to return (1-100)

queryText.description: The search query text to find relevant content in the knowledge base (e.g., "How to implement feature flags in StoreGen?")

ToolSpecification.name: getpackage
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve comprehensive package metadata by package name or ID.
Returns the latest valid revision (SUCCESS or PARTIAL_SUCCESS) with package ID, branch, revisionId, spec type, available files, and associated collections.
Provide exactly one of packageName or packageId. Set includeRevision to false for basic info only (ID, name, branch, creator, description, timestamps).
On package-not-found, the error message points to get-all-packages for discovery.

includeRevision.description: Include latest valid revision metadata and collections (default: true). Set false for basic info only.

ToolSpecification.name: createcollectiondoc
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Creates markdown documentation for a collection. 
This is the primary tool for generating collection documentation. Call this directly - no need to call other tools first for context.

This tool automatically:
- Fetches the collection and its linked elements
- Retrieves the necessary source files from each element
- Returns instructions for generating the documentation

CRITICAL RULES:
- If this tool returns an error, STOP. Do not attempt to create documentation manually.
- NEVER fabricate, assume, or make up documentation content. ALL content must come from actual source files.
- If source files are unavailable, report the error to the user - do NOT create placeholder or assumed content.
- Documentation without real source data is WORTHLESS and potentially harmful.

After generating the content, use the save-spec-studio-doc tool to save it. By default, save-spec-studio-doc auto-increments filenames if they exist (use overwrite=true to replace).
Available doc types: collection-index, collection-overview, collection-architecture, snippet
If no docTypes specified, generates all types.

collectionId.description: The ID of the collection to generate documentation for

docTypes.description: Doc types to generate. If not specified, generates all. Available: collection-index, collection-overview, collection-architecture, snippet

ToolSpecification.name: savespecstudiodoc
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Saves Spec Studio generated markdown documentation to a .md file.

This tool is specifically designed for saving documentation created by Spec Studio tools (e.g., create-collection-doc). Do not use this for saving arbitrary or user-created documents.

If outputPath is not provided, uses the defaultDocPath from user settings (configurable via set_user_setting tool).

If a file with the same name already exists, the previous version is automatically archived to an 'archive' subfolder with a timestamp before saving the new version.

IMPORTANT: After saving, always report the full absolute file path to the user so they know exactly where the file was saved.

outputPath.description: Directory path where the file should be saved (default: current directory)

fileName.description: Name of the output file without extension (default: generated-doc)

ToolSpecification.name: get_user_settings
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieves current user settings from the config file.

Returns the current settings including:
- defaultDocPath: Default directory path for saving generated documentation

If no config file exists, returns sensible defaults.
Config location: ~/.spec-studio-mcp/config.json

ToolSpecification.name: getmyfavorites
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve the authenticated user's favorite packages and collections from Spec Studio.
Returns favorites organized by type (packages and collections).
Optionally filter by entity type to get only packages or only collections.
Use this as a personalized entry point to discover specs the user cares about most.

entityType.description: Optional filter: "package" for packages only, "collection" for collections only

ToolSpecification.name: searchcollections
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Search collections by name (case-insensitive), with optional filtering, sorting, and pagination. If no searchString is provided, returns all collections.

searchString.description: Filter collections by name. If empty, returns all

ToolSpecification.name: getapplicationspec
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Get application metadata and available spec documents by Veritas ID.
Returns collection ID, latest successful revision ID, and a list of available documents.
Use 'get-spec-content' with generationType: COLLECTION to fetch document markdown.

ToolSpecification.name: generatesteeringdoc
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Generates Kiro steering documentation from Spec Studio packages or collections.

For packages:
- Index: {workspaceRoot}/.kiro/steering/PackageName-index.md (always loaded, with key capabilities)
- Docs: {workspaceRoot}/.kiro/specStudio/PackageName-*.md (loaded on-demand via index)
- The index provides categorized documentation with descriptions and key capabilities extracted from the specification
- By default, generates all documentation files for comprehensive Kiro context. Use artifactPaths for specific files
- Note: Only V2 revisions supported. Analysis files excluded

For collections:
- Index: {workspaceRoot}/.kiro/steering/CollectionName-index.md (fileMatch-based loading)
- Docs: {workspaceRoot}/.kiro/specStudio/CollectionName-*.md (fetched from S3, on-demand via links)
- The index provides ecosystem context and loads automatically when working with any package in the collection
- Fetches collection documents from the latest successful collection revision in Spec Studio
- Note: Nested collections must have their docs generated first (bottom-up generation)

Usage:
- { name: "MyService" } - generates package docs (default)
- { name: "MyService", includeAllDocs: false } - only specification.md
- { name: "MyService", artifactPaths: ["docs/api.md"] } - specific files
- { name: "my-collection-123", generationType: "COLLECTION" } - generates collection docs

generationType.description: Type of generated spec: PACKAGE or COLLECTION (default: PACKAGE)

artifactPaths.description: Specific files to generate steering docs from (packages only, e.g., ["docs/api-patterns.md"])

includeAllDocs.description: Generate steering docs from all documentation files (packages only, excludes analysis/*.json files)

ToolSpecification.name: getspecrevisions
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Get and list historical revisions for a specific package or collection. Revisions are returned newest first.
Returns revision history with ID, status, creator, creation date, and additional metadata.
Supports token-based pagination for packages/collections with extensive revision history.
Use for tracking evolution, accessing historical versions, or audit purposes.

generationType.description: Type of generated spec: PACKAGE or COLLECTION (default: PACKAGE)

nextToken.description: Continuation token from previous response for pagination

name.description: Package name or collection ID to search for revisions

ToolSpecification.name: getspeccontent
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from spec-studio-mcp) Retrieve feature content and documentation from specification packages or collection.
V1 packages: Defaults to specification.md if artifactPath not provided.
V2 packages: Requires artifactPath (e.g., "docs/system-overview.md").
Collections: Use artifactPath as document ID.
Returns markdown content of specifications, documentation, and analysis results.

generationType.description: Type of generated spec: PACKAGE or COLLECTION (default: PACKAGE)

revisionId.description: Revision ID. Uses latest valid if not provided

ToolSpecification.name: aws___get_presigned_url
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from aws-mcp) Generate pre-signed S3 URLs for uploading or downloading files. Use this tool BEFORE call_aws when a command requires a local file path (e.g., lambda create-function --zip-file, glacier upload-archive). Use this tool for direct S3 uploads/downloads instead of call_aws (e.g., uploading a file to S3, downloading an object from S3). Maximum upload size is 5 GB (S3 single PUT limit). For files larger than 5 GB, use multipart upload via call_aws instead. For uploads: generates a PUT URL. For downloads: generates a GET URL. By default, no special headers are required — the signature and credentials are embedded in the URL query parameters. However, if s3_params are provided, the corresponding HTTP headers MUST be included in the request or it will fail with SignatureDoesNotMatch.

key.description: S3 object key (path within the bucket).

expires_in.description: URL expiration in seconds (1–900). The maximum is bounded by the FAS session-token lifetime; values above 900 would yield a URL that S3 rejects with 403 ExpiredToken once the token expires.

operation.description: Whether to generate a PUT (upload) or GET (download) URL.

region.description: Override region for the S3 client. Falls back to session region.

s3_params.description: Extra S3 parameters to sign into the URL (e.g. StorageClass, ServerSideEncryption, ContentType). The corresponding HTTP headers MUST be included when using the URL.

ToolSpecification.name: aws___suggest_aws_commands
ToolSpecification.description: (MCP tool from aws-mcp) Suggest AWS CLI commands based on a natural language query. This is a FALLBACK tool to use when you are uncertain about the exact AWS CLI command needed to fulfill a user's request.

query.description: One AWS task in natural language.

ToolSpecification.name: aws___call_aws
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-mcp) Execute AWS CLI commands with validation and proper error handling. This is the PRIMARY tool to use when you are confident about the exact AWS CLI command needed to fulfill a user's request. Always prefer this tool over 'suggest_aws_commands' when you have a specific command in mind.

Key points:
- The command MUST start with "aws" and follow AWS CLI syntax
- For cross-region or account-wide operations, explicitly include --region parameter
- All commands are validated before execution to prevent errors
- The max_results parameter defaults to 100 for paginated operations (e.g., list, describe). Increase it if you need more results, but note that larger values consume more tokens.
- Long-running operations (e.g., CloudFormation stack operations, large S3 transfers) may exceed the timeout and return a task_id with status "working" instead of the normal result
- Use the get_tasks tool, passing the returned task_id in the task_ids parameter, to poll for completion. Wait a few seconds between polls.
- The task continues in the background and is never re-executed - each task_id represents exactly one execution

PAGINATION: If the response includes a non-null "pagination_token", results are truncated. Call again with "--starting-token <pagination_token_value>" to get more results.

IMPORTANT: Local file system access limitation:
- This tool does NOT have access to the local file system since it's executed on a remote MCP server
- For commands that require local file system access (e.g., 'aws s3 cp', 'aws s3 mv', 'aws s3 sync', 'aws s3api put-object', etc.), use the get_presigned_url tool instead to generate a pre-signed S3 URL for upload/download
- S3-to-S3 operations (both source and destination are S3 URIs) ARE supported — e.g., 'aws s3 cp s3://src-bucket/key s3://dst-bucket/key'
- For commands that require an output file argument (e.g., 'aws s3api get-object', 'aws lambda invoke'), use '-' instead of a file path to return data in the response
  Example: aws s3api get-object --bucket <bucket> --key <key> -
- 'file://' and 'fileb://' prefixes are not supported. Provide values directly inline
  Example: aws lambda invoke --function-name <func> --payload '{"key": "value"}' -

Best practices for command generation:
- Always use the most specific service and operation names
- Include --region when operating across regions
- Only use filters (--filters, --query, --prefix, --pattern, etc) when necessary or user explicitly asked for it

Command restrictions:
- DO NOT use bash/zsh pipes (|) or any shell operators
- DO NOT use bash/zsh tools like grep, awk, sed, etc.
- DO NOT use shell redirection operators (>, >>, <)
- DO NOT use command substitution ($(  ))
- DO NOT use shell variables or environment variables
- DO NOT use local file paths as this tool does not have access to the local file system
- DO NOT use 'file://' or 'fileb://' prefixes for input data

Common pitfalls to avoid:
- Missing required parameters - always include all required parameters
- Incorrect parameter values - ensure values match expected format
- Missing --region when operating across regions

Returns: CLI execution results with API response data or error message

FILE UPLOAD WORKFLOW (staging_sources parameter):
When a command requires local files as input, use this workflow:

1. Ask the user which S3 bucket to use for staging file transfers
2. For each file: call get_presigned_url(operation="upload", bucket=<staging_bucket>, key="aws-mcp-staging/<filename>") to get a pre-signed PUT URL
3. Upload each file to its pre-signed URL via HTTP PUT
4. Call call_aws with your CLI command (WITHOUT the file argument flags) + staging_sources
   - The tool downloads all files from S3 in parallel and injects the CLI arguments automatically
   - Do NOT include the cli_argument flags in the cli_command

Each staging_sources entry has fields:
- bucket: the staging bucket name
- key: the staging object key
- cli_argument: the CLI flag to inject (e.g., "--body", "--zip-file", "--template-body")

Pass a list of objects. Max 25 entries per call.

The tool automatically determines the correct file prefix (file://, fileb://, or raw path) based on the CLI argument type.

For commands that natively accept S3 URIs (e.g., lambda create-function --code S3Bucket=X,S3Key=Y), include the S3 reference directly in the CLI command instead of using staging_sources.

Examples:
- S3 upload: aws s3api put-object --bucket my-bucket --key data.bin with staging_sources=[{"bucket":"staging","key":"data.bin","cli_argument":"--body"}]
- Lambda zip: aws lambda create-function --function-name F --runtime python3.12 --handler index.handler --role <arn> with staging_sources=[{"bucket":"staging","key":"func.zip","cli_argument":"--zip-file"}]
- ECS deploy (multiple files): aws ecs deploy --service my-svc --cluster my-cluster with staging_sources=[{"bucket":"staging","key":"task-def.json","cli_argument":"--task-definition"},{"bucket":"staging","key":"appspec.yaml","cli_argument":"--codedeploy-appspec"}]

DIRECTORY UPLOAD WORKFLOW (staging_sources[].extract parameter):
When a command requires a local directory as input (e.g., deploy push --source, gamelift upload-build --build-root):

1. Ask the user which S3 bucket to use for staging
2. Zip the directory (only .zip format is supported)
3. Call get_presigned_url(operation="upload", bucket=<staging_bucket>, key="aws-mcp-staging/archive.zip")
4. Upload the zip to the pre-signed URL via HTTP PUT
5. Call call_aws with staging_sources=[{"bucket":"<staging_bucket>","key":"aws-mcp-staging/archive.zip","cli_argument":"--source","extract":true}]

The server extracts the zip to a temp directory and injects the directory path into the CLI command.
Only .zip format is supported. Max total size (zip + extracted): 4GB.

cli_command.description: The complete AWS CLI command to execute. MUST start with "aws"

items.description: Reference to a file previously uploaded to S3 via get_presigned_url.

anyOf.description:
Required when the CLI command needs local files (e.g. --body, --zip-file, --template-body).
These commands CANNOT use file paths directly — you MUST use staging_sources instead.

**Workflow:**
1. For each file, call get_presigned_url with operation='upload' to get an upload URL
2. Upload each file's content to its URL via HTTP PUT
3. Call call_aws once with staging_sources set to a LIST of objects:
   [{"bucket": "<bucket>", "key": "<key>", "cli_argument": "<param to replace>"}]

**Example — upload a file to S3:**
  cli_command: "aws s3api put-object --bucket my-bucket --key my-file.txt"
  staging_sources: [{"bucket": "staging-bucket", "key": "staged/my-file.txt", "cli_argument": "--body"}]

**Example — ECS deploy (multiple files):**
  cli_command: "aws ecs deploy --service my-svc --cluster my-cluster"
  staging_sources: [
    {"bucket": "staging-bucket", "key": "task-def.json", "cli_argument": "--task-definition"},
    {"bucket": "staging-bucket", "key": "appspec.yaml", "cli_argument": "--codedeploy-appspec"}
  ]

IMPORTANT:
- staging_sources must be a LIST of objects (max 25), each with bucket, key, and cli_argument
- Do NOT include the cli_argument flags in cli_command
- Do NOT pass file paths in cli_command — they will be rejected
- The tool downloads all files from S3 in parallel and injects arguments automatically

max_results.description: Max items for paginated operations (list, describe). Defaults to 100. If --max-items is in the CLI command, the smaller value is used. No effect on non-paginated operations (get, create, delete). If response has a non-null pagination_token, call again with --starting-token to get more.

ToolSpecification.name: aws___get_tasks
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-mcp) Poll the status of long-running tasks started by call_aws or run_script.

WHEN TO USE: After a tool call returns a task_id with status "working", call this tool to check progress. You may poll multiple tasks at once.

LIMITS: Up to 3 task_ids per call. Tasks expire after 5 minutes.

POLLING: Wait a few seconds between polls. Do not poll in a tight loop.

task_ids.description: One or more task IDs (up to 3) returned by previous tool invocations that exceeded the synchronous timeout.

ToolSpecification.name: aws___run_script
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from aws-mcp) Execute Python code in a sandboxed environment with AWS API access via `call_boto3`.

Top-level `await` supported. Imports: stdlib, asyncio. No network access except `call_boto3`.

```python
await call_boto3(service_name='s3', operation_name='ListBuckets',
                 region_name='us-east-1')  # params optional; returns dict, datetimes as ISO strings
```

⚠️ USE run_script FOR:
- 2+ API calls: listing, filtering, counting, parallel calls, multi-step, multi-region
- ANY analysis or comparison: configs, policies, tags, properties across resources
- Permission checks: gather identity, resource, and trust policies in one script
- Streaming APIs (live-tail, subscribe-to-shard) - results auto-capped; inform user if partial.

⚠️ ONE SCRIPT PER TASK: Do NOT split work across multiple run_script calls. If answering the question requires fetching IDs then describing each - do both in one script. Returning intermediate data to "decide the next step" wastes round-trips and inflates the context. Plan the full logic before writing the script.

⚠️ LIST→DESCRIBE IS MANDATORY:
List APIs return ONLY names/ARNs. To check ANY attribute:
1. List* → get ALL names
2. Get*/Describe* PER RESOURCE for the attribute
3. Feature-specific APIs are SEPARATE: streams, encryption, lifecycle, metrics often have dedicated Get* APIs NOT in the main Describe.
4. ResourceNotFoundException = "not configured"-valid data, don't skip

⚠️ CONTENTS vs CONTAINER: "empty buckets" → list objects IN bucket. "records in table" → scan. "X configured" → Get*Configuration API.

⚠️ NEVER TRUNCATE: Check ALL resources. Never `[:10]` or `[:50]`. Missing resource = wrong answer.

⚠️ DISTRUST EMPTY RESULTS: Before reporting "0 found" verify the response had expected keys and no exceptions occurred. If uncertain → "unable to verify" NOT "0 found".

RULES:
1. SELF-CONTAINED: Never paste resource names/ARNs from prior results into code. Discover everything inside the script.
2. OUTPUT: `result = {...}` then `result` alone on the last line. Never use `print()`.
3. CONCURRENCY: `asyncio.gather(*[...], return_exceptions=True)`.
4. REGIONS: The user's default region is used when region_name is not specified. For all-regions: DescribeRegions, iterate ALL.
5. PAGINATION: List/Describe APIs are auto-paginated - do NOT pass limit parameters or loop over tokens manually. Just call the API and all results are returned. Auto-pagination is bypassed only when you explicitly pass the limit key (e.g. `MaxResults`, `Limit`, `MaxItems`) - avoid doing this unless you intentionally want a page.
6. PERMISSIONS: Gather ALL policies in ONE script (identity, resource, trust). ARN `bucket` ≠ `bucket/*`. Report YES, PARTIAL, or NO with reasoning.
7. VERIFY API RESPONSE: Confirm the operation returns the field you need.
8. SUPPRESS EXPECTED ERRORS: When iterating resources where some may not exist, catch and discard expected errors silently. Only propagate unexpected errors. Pattern:
```python
results = await asyncio.gather(*[call_boto3(...) for x in items], return_exceptions=True)
data = [r for r in results if isinstance(r, dict)]
```
9. NO COMMENTS: Do not write any comments in the code.
10. FETCH VS JUDGE: Use the script to fetch and structure data. For mechanical tasks (count, filter by exact value, aggregate) encode the logic in the script. For tasks requiring judgment ("misconfigured", "issues", "best practices", "anomalies") return the relevant raw fields and let the LLM reason. Do not hardcode evaluation heuristics in Python.

IMPORTS: The following modules are pre-imported - do not write any import statements: asyncio, collections, csv, dataclasses, datetime, decimal, enum, fractions, functools, itertools, json, math, re, statistics, string, time, typing, uuid. No other imports are available.

EXAMPLES:

Parallel calls:
```python
buckets, funcs = await asyncio.gather(
    call_boto3(service_name='s3',operation_name='ListBuckets'),
    call_boto3(service_name='lambda',operation_name='ListFunctions'),return_exceptions=True)
result = {
    'buckets':len(buckets.get('Buckets',[])) if isinstance(buckets,dict) else f'ERR:{buckets}',
    'functions':len(funcs.get('Functions',[])) if isinstance(funcs,dict) else f'ERR:{funcs}'}
result
```

Multi-region:
```python
regions = [r['RegionName'] for r in (await call_boto3(service_name='ec2', operation_name='DescribeRegions'))['Regions']]
responses = await asyncio.gather(*[call_boto3(service_name='ec2', operation_name='DescribeInstances', region_name=r) for r in regions], return_exceptions=True)
result = {r: len([i for rv in res.get('Reservations',[]) for i in rv['Instances']]) for r,res in zip(regions,responses) if isinstance(res,dict)}
result
```

List→Describe (auto-paginated - no token loop needed):
```python
r = await call_boto3(service_name='dynamodb', operation_name='ListTables')
tables = r['TableNames']
result = await asyncio.gather(*[call_boto3(service_name='dynamodb',
    operation_name='DescribeKinesisStreamingDestination',
    params={'TableName': t}) for t in tables], return_exceptions=True)
result
```

ToolSpecification.name: add_reactions
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from slack-mcp) Add multiple emoji reactions to a message in parallel (much faster than add_reaction).

Example: { "channel": "#eng", "timestamp": "1234567890.123456", "emojis": ["rocket", "fire", "tada", "star"] }

COMMON SLACK EMOJIS (use these names without colons):
Celebration: tada, confetti_ball, balloon, gift, trophy, medal, crown, star, star2, sparkles, glowing_star
Positive: +1, thumbsup, clap, raised_hands, pray, muscle, fire, 100, heart, heart_eyes, heart_on_fire
Faces: smile, grin, joy, laughing, wink, sunglasses, nerd_face, partying_face, star_struck, exploding_head
Objects: rocket, bulb, dart, gem, crystal_ball, magic_wand, hammer_and_wrench, gear, link, bookmark
Hands: wave, raised_hand, ok_hand, pinched_fingers, crossed_fingers, love_you_gesture, metal, call_me_hand
Status: white_check_mark, heavy_check_mark, x, warning, rotating_light, sos, eyes, thinking_face, question

Slack limits reactions to ~23 per message. Use this tool to add many at once!

emojis.description: Array of emoji names without colons

ToolSpecification.name: get_messages
ToolSpecification.description:
(MCP tool from slack-mcp) Get messages from a channel with full enrichment.
Features: Channel NAME or ID, enriched user details, thread replies, FILE ATTACHMENTS, pagination.
Messages may include a 'files' array with attachment metadata (id, name, mimetype, size, permalink).
To download file content, use the download_file tool with the file ID from the files array.

PERFORMANCE OPTIONS:
- includeThreadReplies: Set to false to skip thread fetching for much faster response (default: true)
- maxThreadsToFetch: Limit threads fetched to reduce API calls (default: 10, max: 50)

Example: { "channel": "#engineering", "limit": 10 }
Fast example: { "channel": "#engineering", "limit": 50, "includeThreadReplies": false }

maxThreadsToFetch.description: Max threads to fetch replies for (default: 10, max: 50)

includeThreadReplies.description: Fetch thread replies (default: true, set false for faster response)
